In 1813, the temple's Yakushi Hall (薬師堂) was rebuilt into a grand structure, housing a {{ill|Yakushi Nyorai|ja|薬師如来|1=Bhaisajyaguru|lt=Yakushi Nyorai (Me)dicine Buddha|12=draft}} statue and the Twelve Divine Generals (薬師十二神将). The temple flourished under the patronage of various regional rulers, and Konpira shrines spread throughout Japan. === Meiji Restoration and the Separation of Shinto and Buddhism === During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|12=draft}}, the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離|12=draft}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) policies led to significant changes at Matsuo-ji. The temple complex was restructured: - The mountain name was changed from Zōzusan (象頭山) to Kotohirayama (琴平山). - The temple village was renamed from Konpira Village (金毘羅村) to Kotohira Village (琴平村). - The shrine was renamed from Konpira Gongen (金毘羅大権現) to {{ill|Kotohira-gū|ja|金刀比羅宮|1=Kotohira-gū|12=draft}}. The temple itself was largely dismantled, with only Fumon-in (普門院) remaining under the name Matsuo-ji. Many Buddhist structures were converted into Shinto shrines or destroyed. However, some Buddhist artifacts, such as a Kamakura-period statue of {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦如来|12=draft}}, were preserved and enshrined in the temple. == Temple Grounds == [[File:Matuojo 20191125 01.jpg|thumb|180px|Main Gate (Sanmon)]] The temple complex includes the following structures: * Sanmon (temple gate) * Main Hall (本堂) * Bell Tower (鐘楼堂) * Kuri (庫裡, temple living quarters) == Cultural Properties == '''Kagawa Prefecture Designated Cultural Properties''' * '''Seated Statue of Kōbō Daishi''' (木造弘法大師座像) – Created in 1319 (Bunpō 3), made of cypress wood (檜材), designated on March 31, 2009. * '''Wooden Five-Ring Pagoda''' (木造五輪塔) – Contains an inscription dated 1604 (Keichō 9), associated with the monk Yūsei (宥盛), designated on March 31, 2009. == location == * '''Address:''' 973 Kawanishi, Kotohira, Nakatado District, Kagawa Prefecture, Japan (香川県仲多度郡琴平町字川西973番地) == Pilgrimage == * '''Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage''' ''' 15: {{ill|Kenshō-ji (Marugame)|ja|顕正寺|12=draft}} → '''16: Matsuo-ji''' → 17: {{ill|Butsumo-in|ja|仏母院|12=draft}} == References == === Notes === {{Notelist}} === Sources === {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Konpira Gongen|ja|金毘羅権現|12=draft}} == External Links == * [http://www.mandala88.com/ Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage Website] {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| }} {{translated page|ja|松尾寺 (香川県琴平町)|version=98510282|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Buddhist temples in Kagawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Kōyasan Shingon temples]] [[Category:En no Gyōja]] [[Category:Shinbutsu-shūgō]] [[Category:Betto Temples]] [[Category:Konpira Shrine|Matsuo-ji]] [[Category:Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage]] [[Category:Kotohira, Kagawa]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Kotohira shrines]] }} [[zh:松尾寺 (琴平町)]] [[en:Matsuo-ji]] [[ja:%E6%9D%BE%E5%B0%BE%E5%AF%BA (%E9%A6%99%E5%B7%9D%E7%9C%8C%E7%90%B4%E5%B9%B3%E7%94%BA)]] [[ja:&action=edit]] [[sn:Matsuo-ji]]

{{Japanese temple infobox | Name = Matsuo-ji (松尾寺) | image = [[File:Matuozi02.jpg|250px]]
Main Hall | location = {{ill|Kotohira, Kagawa|ja|琴平町|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nakatado District, Kagawa|ja|仲多度郡|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kagawa Prefecture|ja|香川県|12=draft}} | coordinates = {{Coord|34|11|8.8|N|133|49|6.2|E}} | Mount name = Zōzusan (象頭山) | Sect = {{ill|Kōyasan Shingon-shū|ja|高野山真言宗|12=draft}} | Main deity = {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦如来|12=draft}} | Founded = {{ill|Taihō era|ja|大宝|12=draft}} (701–704 AD) | Founder = {{ill|En no Gyōja|ja|役小角|12=draft}} | Official name = Zōzusan Fumon-in Matsuo-ji (象頭山 普門院 松尾寺) | Pilgrimage = {{ill|Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|ja|新四国曼荼羅霊場|12=draft}} No.16 }} '''Matsuo-ji''' (松尾寺) is a {{ill|Kōyasan Shingon-shū|ja|高野山真言宗|12=draft}} temple located in {{ill|Kotohira, Kagawa|ja|琴平町|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nakatado District, Kagawa|ja|仲多度郡|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kagawa Prefecture|ja|香川県|12=draft}}. The temple's mountain name (山号) is Zōzusan (象頭山), and its cloister name (院号) is Fumon-in (普門院). The principal deity is {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦如来|12=draft}}, and it is the 16th temple in the {{ill|Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|ja|新四国曼荼羅霊場|12=draft}}. '''Temple hymn (Goeika, ご詠歌):''' *"Miagureba / kokoro mo kiyoki / Zōzusan / kin no gohei ni / kami zo masumasu"* (*"Looking up, / even the heart is purified, / Zōzusan, / with golden gohei, / the deity is ever-present."*) == History == [[File:Matuoji 20191125 02.jpg|thumb|180px|Sanmon (temple gate)]] According to the temple's legend, Matsuo-ji was founded in the {{ill|Taihō era|ja|大宝|12=draft}} (701–704 AD) when {{ill|En no Gyōja|ja|役小角|12=draft}}, the founder of {{ill|Shugendō|ja|修験道|12=draft}}, climbed Mount Zōzusan. It is said that he received a divine revelation from {{ill|Kumbhīra (mythology)|ja|マカラ|12=draft}}, a protective deity (護法善神) dwelling in {{ill|Vulture Peak|ja|霊鷲山|12=draft}} in India, which later became associated with {{ill|Konpira Gongen|ja|金毘羅権現|12=draft}}.Short history of Matsuo-ji However, many historical records were lost during the invasion of {{ill|Chōsokabe Motochika|ja|長宗我部元親|12=draft}} into {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国|12=draft}}. The earliest records of Konpira Gongen appear only in the early modern period. Before that, Matsuo-ji had its main hall at the current site of Hongū (本宮), enshrining an Eleven-Headed Kannon (十一面観音菩薩) as its principal deity. A shrine to the Thirty Guardian Deities (三十番神) was also located nearby. During the late 16th century, Matsuo-ji underwent significant transformations. A monk named Yūga (宥雅), a disciple of {{ill|Zentsū-ji|ja|善通寺|12=draft}}, became the temple's abbot and founded Konkō-in (金光院). In 1573, he established a hall for Konpira Gongen (金毘羅堂) at the foot of the temple complex, separate from the original temple structure. In 1579, when {{ill|Chōsokabe Motochika|ja|長宗我部元親|12=draft}} sought to control religious institutions in Sanuki, Yūga fled, and the Chōsokabe forces appointed the monk Yūgen (宥厳) as the new abbot. During the Edo period, Konpira Gongen's faith expanded, and Matsuo-ji became a prominent center of pilgrimage. In 1813, the temple's Yakushi Hall (薬師堂) was rebuilt into a grand structure, housing a {{ill|Yakushi Nyorai|ja|薬師如来|1=Bhaisajyaguru|lt=Yakushi Nyorai (Me)dicine Buddha|12=draft}} statue and the Twelve Divine Generals (薬師十二神将). The temple flourished under the patronage of various regional rulers, and Konpira shrines spread throughout Japan. === Meiji Restoration and the Separation of Shinto and Buddhism === During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|12=draft}}, the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離|12=draft}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) policies led to significant changes at Matsuo-ji. The temple complex was restructured: - The mountain name was changed from Zōzusan (象頭山) to Kotohirayama (琴平山). - The temple village was renamed from Konpira Village (金毘羅村) to Kotohira Village (琴平村). - The shrine was renamed from Konpira Gongen (金毘羅大権現) to {{ill|Kotohira-gū|ja|金刀比羅宮|1=Kotohira-gū|12=draft}}. The temple itself was largely dismantled, with only Fumon-in (普門院) remaining under the name Matsuo-ji. Many Buddhist structures were converted into Shinto shrines or destroyed. However, some Buddhist artifacts, such as a Kamakura-period statue of {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦如来|12=draft}}, were preserved and enshrined in the temple. == Temple Grounds == [[File:Matuojo 20191125 01.jpg|thumb|180px|Main Gate (Sanmon)]] The temple complex includes the following structures: * Sanmon (temple gate) * Main Hall (本堂) * Bell Tower (鐘楼堂) * Kuri (庫裡, temple living quarters) == Cultural Properties == '''Kagawa Prefecture Designated Cultural Properties''' * '''Seated Statue of Kōbō Daishi''' (木造弘法大師座像) – Created in 1319 (Bunpō 3), made of cypress wood (檜材), designated on March 31, 2009. * '''Wooden Five-Ring Pagoda''' (木造五輪塔) – Contains an inscription dated 1604 (Keichō 9), associated with the monk Yūsei (宥盛), designated on March 31, 2009. == location == * '''Address:''' 973 Kawanishi, Kotohira, Nakatado District, Kagawa Prefecture, Japan (香川県仲多度郡琴平町字川西973番地) == Pilgrimage == * '''Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage''' ''' 15: {{ill|Kenshō-ji (Marugame)|ja|顕正寺|12=draft}} → '''16: Matsuo-ji''' → 17: {{ill|Butsumo-in|ja|仏母院|12=draft}} == References == === Notes === {{Notelist}} === Sources === {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Konpira Gongen|ja|金毘羅権現|12=draft}} == External Links == * [http://www.mandala88.com/ Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage Website] {{translated page|ja|松尾寺 (香川県琴平町)|version=98510282|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Buddhist temples in Kagawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Kōyasan Shingon temples]] [[Category:En no Gyōja]] [[Category:Shinbutsu-shūgō]] [[Category:Betto Temples]] [[Category:Konpira Shrine|Matsuo-ji]] [[Category:Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage]] [[Category:Kotohira, Kagawa]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Kotohira shrines]] }} [[zh:松尾寺 (琴平町)]] [[en:Matsuo-ji]] [[ja:%E6%9D%BE%E5%B0%BE%E5%AF%BA (%E9%A6%99%E5%B7%9D%E7%9C%8C%E7%90%B4%E5%B9%B3%E7%94%BA)]] [[ja:&action=edit]] [[sn:Matsuo-ji]]

The temple complex was restructured: - The mountain name was changed from Zōzusan (象頭山) to Kotohirayama (琴平山). - The temple village was renamed from Konpira Village (金毘羅村) to Kotohira Village (琴平村). - The shrine was renamed from Konpira Gongen (金毘羅大権現) to {{ill|Kotohira-gū|ja|金刀比羅宮|1=Kotohira-gū|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The temple itself was largely dismantled, with only Fumon-in (普門院) remaining under the name Matsuo-ji. Many Buddhist structures were converted into Shinto shrines or destroyed. However, some Buddhist artifacts, such as a Kamakura-period statue of {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦如来|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, were preserved and enshrined in the temple. == Temple Grounds == [[File:Matuojo 20191125 01.jpg|thumb|180px|Main Gate (Sanmon)]] The temple complex includes the following structures: * Sanmon (temple gate) * Main Hall (本堂) * Bell Tower (鐘楼堂) * Kuri (庫裡, temple living quarters) == Cultural Properties == '''Kagawa Prefecture Designated Cultural Properties''' * '''Seated Statue of Kōbō Daishi''' (木造弘法大師座像) – Created in 1319 (Bunpō 3), made of cypress wood (檜材), designated on March 31, 2009. * '''Wooden Five-Ring Pagoda''' (木造五輪塔) – Contains an inscription dated 1604 (Keichō 9), associated with the monk Yūsei (宥盛), designated on March 31, 2009. == location == * '''Address:''' 973 Kawanishi, Kotohira, Nakatado District, Kagawa Prefecture, Japan (香川県仲多度郡琴平町字川西973番地) == Pilgrimage == * '''Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage''' ''' 15: {{ill|Kenshō-ji (Marugame)|ja|顕正寺|qq=draft|qq=draft}} → '''16: Matsuo-ji''' → 17: {{ill|Butsumo-in|ja|仏母院|qq=draft|qq=draft}} == References == === Notes === {{Notelist}} === Sources === {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Konpira Gongen|ja|金毘羅権現|qq=draft|qq=draft}} == External Links == * [http://www.mandala88.com/ Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage Website] {{translated page|ja|松尾寺 (香川県琴平町)|version=98510282|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Buddhist temples in Kagawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Kōyasan Shingon temples]] [[Category:En no Gyōja]] [[Category:Shinbutsu-shūgō]] [[Category:Betto Temples]] [[Category:Konpira Shrine|Matsuo-ji]] [[Category:Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage]] [[Category:Kotohira, Kagawa]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Kotohira shrines]] }} [[zh:松尾寺 (琴平町)]] [[en:Matsuo-ji]] [[ja:%E6%9D%BE%E5%B0%BE%E5%AF%BA (%E9%A6%99%E5%B7%9D%E7%9C%8C%E7%90%B4%E5%B9%B3%E7%94%BA)]] [[ja:&action=edit]] [[sn:Matsuo-ji]]

revision.text:
{{Japanese temple infobox | Name = Matsuo-ji (松尾寺) | image = [[File:Matuozi02.jpg|250px]]
Main Hall | location = {{ill|Kotohira, Kagawa|ja|琴平町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotohira, Kagawa|qq=}}, {{ill|Nakatado District, Kagawa|ja|仲多度郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatado District, Kagawa|qq=}}, {{ill|Kagawa Prefecture|ja|香川県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagawa Prefecture|qq=}} | coordinates = {{Coord|34|11|8.8|N|133|49|6.2|E}} | Mount name = Zōzusan (象頭山) | Sect = {{ill|Kōyasan Shingon-shū|ja|高野山真言宗|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōyasan Shingon-shū|qq=}} | Main deity = {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦如来|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni|qq=}} | Founded = {{ill|Taihō era|ja|大宝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taihō era|qq=}} (701–704 AD) | Founder = {{ill|En no Gyōja|ja|役小角|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/En no Gyōja|qq=}} | Official name = Zōzusan Fumon-in Matsuo-ji (象頭山 普門院 松尾寺) | Pilgrimage = {{ill|Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|ja|新四国曼荼羅霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|qq=}} No.16 }} '''Matsuo-ji''' (松尾寺) is a {{ill|Kōyasan Shingon-shū|ja|高野山真言宗|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōyasan Shingon-shū|qq=}} temple located in {{ill|Kotohira, Kagawa|ja|琴平町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotohira, Kagawa|qq=}}, {{ill|Nakatado District, Kagawa|ja|仲多度郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatado District, Kagawa|qq=}}, {{ill|Kagawa Prefecture|ja|香川県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagawa Prefecture|qq=}}. The temple's mountain name (山号) is Zōzusan (象頭山), and its cloister name (院号) is Fumon-in (普門院). The principal deity is {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦如来|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni|qq=}}, and it is the 16th temple in the {{ill|Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|ja|新四国曼荼羅霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|qq=}}. '''Temple hymn (Goeika, ご詠歌):''' *"Miagureba / kokoro mo kiyoki / Zōzusan / kin no gohei ni / kami zo masumasu"* (*"Looking up, / even the heart is purified, / Zōzusan, / with golden gohei, / the deity is ever-present."*) == History == [[File:Matuoji 20191125 02.jpg|thumb|180px|Sanmon (temple gate)]] According to the temple's legend, Matsuo-ji was founded in the {{ill|Taihō era|ja|大宝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taihō era|qq=}} (701–704 AD) when {{ill|En no Gyōja|ja|役小角|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/En no Gyōja|qq=}}, the founder of {{ill|Shugendō|ja|修験道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shugendō|qq=}}, climbed Mount Zōzusan. It is said that he received a divine revelation from {{ill|Kumbhīra (mythology)|ja|マカラ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumbhīra (mythology)|qq=}}, a protective deity (護法善神) dwelling in {{ill|Vulture Peak|ja|霊鷲山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Vulture Peak|qq=}} in India, which later became associated with {{ill|Konpira Gongen|ja|金毘羅権現|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konpira Gongen|qq=}}.Short history of Matsuo-ji However, many historical records were lost during the invasion of {{ill|Chōsokabe Motochika|ja|長宗我部元親|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōsokabe Motochika|qq=}} into {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanuki Province|qq=}}. The earliest records of Konpira Gongen appear only in the early modern period. Before that, Matsuo-ji had its main hall at the current site of Hongū (本宮), enshrining an Eleven-Headed Kannon (十一面観音菩薩) as its principal deity. A shrine to the Thirty Guardian Deities (三十番神) was also located nearby. During the late 16th century, Matsuo-ji underwent significant transformations. A monk named Yūga (宥雅), a disciple of {{ill|Zentsū-ji|ja|善通寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zentsū-ji|qq=}}, became the temple's abbot and founded Konkō-in (金光院). In 1573, he established a hall for Konpira Gongen (金毘羅堂) at the foot of the temple complex, separate from the original temple structure. In 1579, when {{ill|Chōsokabe Motochika|ja|長宗我部元親|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōsokabe Motochika|qq=}} sought to control religious institutions in Sanuki, Yūga fled, and the Chōsokabe forces appointed the monk Yūgen (宥厳) as the new abbot. During the Edo period, Konpira Gongen's faith expanded, and Matsuo-ji became a prominent center of pilgrimage. In 1813, the temple's Yakushi Hall (薬師堂) was rebuilt into a grand structure, housing a {{ill|Yakushi Nyorai|ja|薬師如来|1=Bhaisajyaguru|lt=Yakushi Nyorai (Me)dicine Buddha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yakushi Nyorai|13=User:Immanuelle/Bhaisajyaguru|qq=}} statue and the Twelve Divine Generals (薬師十二神将). The temple flourished under the patronage of various regional rulers, and Konpira shrines spread throughout Japan. === Meiji Restoration and the Separation of Shinto and Buddhism === During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji Restoration|qq=}}, the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu bunri|qq=}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) policies led to significant changes at Matsuo-ji. The temple complex was restructured: - The mountain name was changed from Zōzusan (象頭山) to Kotohirayama (琴平山). - The temple village was renamed from Konpira Village (金毘羅村) to Kotohira Village (琴平村). - The shrine was renamed from Konpira Gongen (金毘羅大権現) to {{ill|Kotohira-gū|ja|金刀比羅宮|1=Kotohira-gū|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotohira-gū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotohira-gū|qq=}}. The temple itself was largely dismantled, with only Fumon-in (普門院) remaining under the name Matsuo-ji. Many Buddhist structures were converted into Shinto shrines or destroyed. However, some Buddhist artifacts, such as a Kamakura-period statue of {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦如来|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni|qq=}}, were preserved and enshrined in the temple. == Temple Grounds == [[File:Matuojo 20191125 01.jpg|thumb|180px|Main Gate (Sanmon)]] The temple complex includes the following structures: * Sanmon (temple gate) * Main Hall (本堂) * Bell Tower (鐘楼堂) * Kuri (庫裡, temple living quarters) == Cultural Properties == '''Kagawa Prefecture Designated Cultural Properties''' * '''Seated Statue of Kōbō Daishi''' (木造弘法大師座像) – Created in 1319 (Bunpō 3), made of cypress wood (檜材), designated on March 31, 2009. * '''Wooden Five-Ring Pagoda''' (木造五輪塔) – Contains an inscription dated 1604 (Keichō 9), associated with the monk Yūsei (宥盛), designated on March 31, 2009. == location == * '''Address:''' 973 Kawanishi, Kotohira, Nakatado District, Kagawa Prefecture, Japan (香川県仲多度郡琴平町字川西973番地) == Pilgrimage == * '''Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage''' ''' 15: {{ill|Kenshō-ji (Marugame)|ja|顕正寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenshō-ji (Marugame)|qq=}} → '''16: Matsuo-ji''' → 17: {{ill|Butsumo-in|ja|仏母院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Butsumo-in|qq=}} == References == === Notes === {{Notelist}} === Sources === {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Konpira Gongen|ja|金毘羅権現|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konpira Gongen|qq=}} == External Links == * [http://www.mandala88.com/ Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage Website] {{translated page|ja|松尾寺 (香川県琴平町)|version=98510282|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Buddhist temples in Kagawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Kōyasan Shingon temples]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Kotohira shrines]] }} [[zh:松尾寺 (琴平町)]] [[en:Matsuo-ji]] [[ja:%E6%9D%BE%E5%B0%BE%E5%AF%BA (%E9%A6%99%E5%B7%9D%E7%9C%8C%E7%90%B4%E5%B9%B3%E7%94%BA)]] {{Japanese temple infobox | Name = Matsuo-ji (松尾寺) | image = [[File:Matuozi02.jpg|250px]]
Main Hall | location = {{ill|Kotohira, Kagawa|ja|琴平町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotohira, Kagawa|qq=}}, {{ill|Nakatado District, Kagawa|ja|仲多度郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatado District, Kagawa|qq=}}, {{ill|Kagawa Prefecture|ja|香川県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagawa Prefecture|qq=}} | coordinates = {{Coord|34|11|8.8|N|133|49|6.2|E}} | Mount name = Zōzusan (象頭山) | Sect = {{ill|Kōyasan Shingon-shū|ja|高野山真言宗|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōyasan Shingon-shū|qq=}} | Main deity = {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦如来|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni|qq=}} | Founded = {{ill|Taihō era|ja|大宝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taihō era|qq=}} (701–704 AD) | Founder = {{ill|En no Gyōja|ja|役小角|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/En no Gyōja|qq=}} | Official name = Zōzusan Fumon-in Matsuo-ji (象頭山 普門院 松尾寺) | Pilgrimage = {{ill|Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|ja|新四国曼荼羅霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|qq=}} No.16 }} '''Matsuo-ji''' (松尾寺) is a {{ill|Kōyasan Shingon-shū|ja|高野山真言宗|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōyasan Shingon-shū|qq=}} temple located in {{ill|Kotohira, Kagawa|ja|琴平町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotohira, Kagawa|qq=}}, {{ill|Nakatado District, Kagawa|ja|仲多度郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatado District, Kagawa|qq=}}, {{ill|Kagawa Prefecture|ja|香川県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagawa Prefecture|qq=}}. The temple's mountain name (山号) is Zōzusan (象頭山), and its cloister name (院号) is Fumon-in (普門院). The principal deity is {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦如来|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni|qq=}}, and it is the 16th temple in the {{ill|Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|ja|新四国曼荼羅霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|qq=}}. '''Temple hymn (Goeika, ご詠歌):''' *"Miagureba / kokoro mo kiyoki / Zōzusan / kin no gohei ni / kami zo masumasu"* (*"Looking up, / even the heart is purified, / Zōzusan, / with golden gohei, / the deity is ever-present."*) == History == [[File:Matuoji 20191125 02.jpg|thumb|180px|Sanmon (temple gate)]] According to the temple's legend, Matsuo-ji was founded in the {{ill|Taihō era|ja|大宝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taihō era|qq=}} (701–704 AD) when {{ill|En no Gyōja|ja|役小角|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/En no Gyōja|qq=}}, the founder of {{ill|Shugendō|ja|修験道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shugendō|qq=}}, climbed Mount Zōzusan. It is said that he received a divine revelation from {{ill|Kumbhīra (mythology)|ja|マカラ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumbhīra (mythology)|qq=}}, a protective deity (護法善神) dwelling in {{ill|Vulture Peak|ja|霊鷲山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Vulture Peak|qq=}} in India, which later became associated with {{ill|Konpira Gongen|ja|金毘羅権現|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konpira Gongen|qq=}}.Short history of Matsuo-ji However, many historical records were lost during the invasion of {{ill|Chōsokabe Motochika|ja|長宗我部元親|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōsokabe Motochika|qq=}} into {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanuki Province|qq=}}. The earliest records of Konpira Gongen appear only in the early modern period. Before that, Matsuo-ji had its main hall at the current site of Hongū (本宮), enshrining an Eleven-Headed Kannon (十一面観音菩薩) as its principal deity. A shrine to the Thirty Guardian Deities (三十番神) was also located nearby. During the late 16th century, Matsuo-ji underwent significant transformations. A monk named Yūga (宥雅), a disciple of {{ill|Zentsū-ji|ja|善通寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zentsū-ji|qq=}}, became the temple's abbot and founded Konkō-in (金光院). In 1573, he established a hall for Konpira Gongen (金毘羅堂) at the foot of the temple complex, separate from the original temple structure. In 1579, when {{ill|Chōsokabe Motochika|ja|長宗我部元親|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōsokabe Motochika|qq=}} sought to control religious institutions in Sanuki, Yūga fled, and the Chōsokabe forces appointed the monk Yūgen (宥厳) as the new abbot. During the Edo period, Konpira Gongen's faith expanded, and Matsuo-ji became a prominent center of pilgrimage. In 1813, the temple's Yakushi Hall (薬師堂) was rebuilt into a grand structure, housing a {{ill|Yakushi Nyorai|ja|薬師如来|1=Bhaisajyaguru|lt=Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yakushi Nyorai|13=User:Immanuelle/Bhaisajyaguru|qq=}} statue and the Twelve Divine Generals (薬師十二神将). The temple flourished under the patronage of various regional rulers, and Konpira shrines spread throughout Japan. === Meiji Restoration and the Separation of Shinto and Buddhism === During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji Restoration|qq=}}, the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu bunri|qq=}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) policies led to significant changes at Matsuo-ji. The temple complex was restructured: - The mountain name was changed from Zōzusan (象頭山) to Kotohirayama (琴平山). - The temple village was renamed from Konpira Village (金毘羅村) to Kotohira Village (琴平村). - The shrine was renamed from Konpira Gongen (金毘羅大権現) to {{ill|Kotohira-gū|ja|金刀比羅宮|1=Kotohira-gū|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotohira-gū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotohira-gū|qq=}}. The temple itself was largely dismantled, with only Fumon-in (普門院) remaining under the name Matsuo-ji. Many Buddhist structures were converted into Shinto shrines or destroyed. However, some Buddhist artifacts, such as a Kamakura-period statue of {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦如来|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni|qq=}}, were preserved and enshrined in the temple. == Temple Grounds == [[File:Matuojo 20191125 01.jpg|thumb|180px|Main Gate (Sanmon)]] The temple complex includes the following structures: * Sanmon (temple gate) * Main Hall (本堂) * Bell Tower (鐘楼堂) * Kuri (庫裡, temple living quarters) == Cultural Properties == '''Kagawa Prefecture Designated Cultural Properties''' * '''Seated Statue of Kōbō Daishi''' (木造弘法大師座像) – Created in 1319 (Bunpō 3), made of cypress wood (檜材), designated on March 31, 2009. * '''Wooden Five-Ring Pagoda''' (木造五輪塔) – Contains an inscription dated 1604 (Keichō 9), associated with the monk Yūsei (宥盛), designated on March 31, 2009. == location == * '''Address:''' 973 Kawanishi, Kotohira, Nakatado District, Kagawa Prefecture, Japan (香川県仲多度郡琴平町字川西973番地) == Pilgrimage == * '''Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage''' ''' 15: {{ill|Kenshō-ji (Marugame)|ja|顕正寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenshō-ji (Marugame)|qq=}} → '''16: Matsuo-ji''' → 17: {{ill|Butsumo-in|ja|仏母院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Butsumo-in|qq=}} == References == === Notes === {{Notelist}} === Sources === {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Konpira Gongen|ja|金毘羅権現|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konpira Gongen|qq=}} == External Links == * [http://www.mandala88.com/ Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage Website] {{translated page|ja|松尾寺 (香川県琴平町)|version=98510282|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Buddhist temples in Kagawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Kōyasan Shingon temples]] [[Category:En no Gyōja]] [[Category:Shinbutsu-shūgō]] [[Category:Konpira Shrine|Matsuo-ji]] [[Category:Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage]] [[Category:Kotohira, Kagawa]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Kotohira shrines]] }} [[zh:松尾寺 (琴平町)]] [[en:Matsuo-ji]] [[ja:%E6%9D%BE%E5%B0%BE%E5%AF%BA (%E9%A6%99%E5%B7%9D%E7%9C%8C%E7%90%B4%E5%B9%B3%E7%94%BA)]] [[ja:&action=edit]] [[sn:Matsuo-ji]]

In 1813, the temple's Yakushi Hall (薬師堂) was rebuilt into a grand structure, housing a [[:en:Bhaisajyaguru|Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha]] statue and the Twelve Divine Generals (薬師十二神将). The temple flourished under the patronage of various regional rulers, and Konpira shrines spread throughout Japan. === Meiji Restoration and the Separation of Shinto and Buddhism === During the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) policies led to significant changes at Matsuo-ji. The temple complex was restructured: - The mountain name was changed from Zōzusan (象頭山) to Kotohirayama (琴平山). - The temple village was renamed from Konpira Village (金毘羅村) to Kotohira Village (琴平村). - The shrine was renamed from Konpira Gongen (金毘羅大権現) to [[:en:Kotohira-gū|Kotohira-gū]]. The temple itself was largely dismantled, with only Fumon-in (普門院) remaining under the name Matsuo-ji. Many Buddhist structures were converted into Shinto shrines or destroyed. However, some Buddhist artifacts, such as a Kamakura-period statue of {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦如来|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni|qq=}}, were preserved and enshrined in the temple. == Temple Grounds == [[File:Matuojo 20191125 01.jpg|thumb|180px|Main Gate (Sanmon)]] The temple complex includes the following structures: * Sanmon (temple gate) * Main Hall (本堂) * Bell Tower (鐘楼堂) * Kuri (庫裡, temple living quarters) == Cultural Properties == '''Kagawa Prefecture Designated Cultural Properties''' * '''Seated Statue of Kōbō Daishi''' (木造弘法大師座像) – Created in 1319 (Bunpō 3), made of cypress wood (檜材), designated on March 31, 2009. * '''Wooden Five-Ring Pagoda''' (木造五輪塔) – Contains an inscription dated 1604 (Keichō 9), associated with the monk Yūsei (宥盛), designated on March 31, 2009. == location == * '''Address:''' 973 Kawanishi, Kotohira, Nakatado District, Kagawa Prefecture, Japan (香川県仲多度郡琴平町字川西973番地) == Pilgrimage == * '''Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage''' ''' 15: {{ill|Kenshō-ji (Marugame)|ja|顕正寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenshō-ji (Marugame)|qq=}} → '''16: Matsuo-ji''' → 17: {{ill|Butsumo-in|ja|仏母院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Butsumo-in|qq=}} == References == === Notes === {{Notelist}} === Sources === {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[:en:Konpira Gongen|Konpira Gongen]] == External Links == * [http://www.mandala88.com/ Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage Website] {{translated page|ja|松尾寺 (香川県琴平町)|version=98510282|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Buddhist temples in Kagawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Kōyasan Shingon temples]] [[Category:En no Gyōja]] [[Category:Shinbutsu-shūgō]] [[Category:Konpira Shrine|Matsuo-ji]] [[Category:Kotohira, Kagawa]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Kotohira shrines]] }} [[zh:松尾寺 (琴平町)]] [[en:Matsuo-ji]] [[ja:%E6%9D%BE%E5%B0%BE%E5%AF%BA (%E9%A6%99%E5%B7%9D%E7%9C%8C%E7%90%B4%E5%B9%B3%E7%94%BA)]] [[ja:&action=edit]] [[sn:Matsuo-ji]]

{{Japanese temple infobox | Name = Matsuo-ji (松尾寺) | image = [[File:Matuozi02.jpg|250px]]
Main Hall | location = [[:en:Kotohira, Kagawa|Kotohira, Kagawa]], [[:en:Nakatado District, Kagawa|Nakatado District, Kagawa]], [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{Coord|34|11|8.8|N|133|49|6.2|E}} | Mount name = Zōzusan (象頭山) | Sect = [[:en:Kōyasan Shingon-shū|Kōyasan Shingon-shū]] | Main deity = {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦如来|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni|qq=}} | Founded = [[:en:Taiho|Taihō era]] (701–704 AD) | Founder = [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]] | Official name = Zōzusan Fumon-in Matsuo-ji (象頭山 普門院 松尾寺) | Pilgrimage = {{ill|Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|ja|新四国曼荼羅霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|qq=}} No.16 }} '''Matsuo-ji''' (松尾寺) is a [[:en:Kōyasan Shingon-shū|Kōyasan Shingon-shū]] temple located in [[:en:Kotohira, Kagawa|Kotohira, Kagawa]], [[:en:Nakatado District, Kagawa|Nakatado District, Kagawa]], [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]]. The temple's mountain name (山号) is Zōzusan (象頭山), and its cloister name (院号) is Fumon-in (普門院). The principal deity is {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦如来|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni|qq=}}, and it is the 16th temple in the {{ill|Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|ja|新四国曼荼羅霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|qq=}}. '''Temple hymn (Goeika, ご詠歌):''' *"Miagureba / kokoro mo kiyoki / Zōzusan / kin no gohei ni / kami zo masumasu"* (*"Looking up, / even the heart is purified, / Zōzusan, / with golden gohei, / the deity is ever-present."*) == History == [[File:Matuoji 20191125 02.jpg|thumb|180px|Sanmon (temple gate)]] According to the temple's legend, Matsuo-ji was founded in the [[:en:Taiho|Taihō era]] (701–704 AD) when [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]], the founder of [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendō]], climbed Mount Zōzusan. It is said that he received a divine revelation from {{ill|Kumbhīra (mythology)|ja|マカラ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumbhīra (mythology)|qq=}}, a protective deity (護法善神) dwelling in [[:en:Vulture Peak|Vulture Peak]] in India, which later became associated with [[:en:Konpira Gongen|Konpira Gongen]].Short history of Matsuo-ji However, many historical records were lost during the invasion of [[:en:Chōsokabe Motochika|Chōsokabe Motochika]] into [[:en:Sanuki Province|Sanuki Province]]. The earliest records of Konpira Gongen appear only in the early modern period. Before that, Matsuo-ji had its main hall at the current site of Hongū (本宮), enshrining an Eleven-Headed Kannon (十一面観音菩薩) as its principal deity. A shrine to the Thirty Guardian Deities (三十番神) was also located nearby. During the late 16th century, Matsuo-ji underwent significant transformations. A monk named Yūga (宥雅), a disciple of [[:en:Zentsū-ji|Zentsū-ji]], became the temple's abbot and founded Konkō-in (金光院). In 1573, he established a hall for Konpira Gongen (金毘羅堂) at the foot of the temple complex, separate from the original temple structure. In 1579, when [[:en:Chōsokabe Motochika|Chōsokabe Motochika]] sought to control religious institutions in Sanuki, Yūga fled, and the Chōsokabe forces appointed the monk Yūgen (宥厳) as the new abbot. During the Edo period, Konpira Gongen's faith expanded, and Matsuo-ji became a prominent center of pilgrimage. In 1813, the temple's Yakushi Hall (薬師堂) was rebuilt into a grand structure, housing a [[:en:Bhaisajyaguru|Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha]] statue and the Twelve Divine Generals (薬師十二神将). The temple flourished under the patronage of various regional rulers, and Konpira shrines spread throughout Japan. === Meiji Restoration and the Separation of Shinto and Buddhism === During the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) policies led to significant changes at Matsuo-ji. The temple complex was restructured: - The mountain name was changed from Zōzusan (象頭山) to Kotohirayama (琴平山). - The temple village was renamed from Konpira Village (金毘羅村) to Kotohira Village (琴平村). - The shrine was renamed from Konpira Gongen (金毘羅大権現) to [[:en:Kotohira-gū|Kotohira-gū]]. The temple itself was largely dismantled, with only Fumon-in (普門院) remaining under the name Matsuo-ji. Many Buddhist structures were converted into Shinto shrines or destroyed. However, some Buddhist artifacts, such as a Kamakura-period statue of {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦如来|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni|qq=}}, were preserved and enshrined in the temple. == Temple Grounds == [[File:Matuojo 20191125 01.jpg|thumb|180px|Main Gate (Sanmon)]] The temple complex includes the following structures: * Sanmon (temple gate) * Main Hall (本堂) * Bell Tower (鐘楼堂) * Kuri (庫裡, temple living quarters) == Cultural Properties == '''Kagawa Prefecture Designated Cultural Properties''' * '''Seated Statue of Kōbō Daishi''' (木造弘法大師座像) – Created in 1319 (Bunpō 3), made of cypress wood (檜材), designated on March 31, 2009. * '''Wooden Five-Ring Pagoda''' (木造五輪塔) – Contains an inscription dated 1604 (Keichō 9), associated with the monk Yūsei (宥盛), designated on March 31, 2009. == location == * '''Address:''' 973 Kawanishi, Kotohira, Nakatado District, Kagawa Prefecture, Japan (香川県仲多度郡琴平町字川西973番地) == Pilgrimage == * '''Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage''' ''' 15: revision.text:
{{Japanese temple infobox | Name = Matsuo-ji (松尾寺) | image = [[File:Matuozi02.jpg|250px]]
Main Hall | location = [[:en:Kotohira, Kagawa|Kotohira, Kagawa]], [[:en:Nakatado District, Kagawa|Nakatado District, Kagawa]], [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{Coord|34|11|8.8|N|133|49|6.2|E}} | Mount name = Zōzusan (象頭山) | Sect = [[:en:Kōyasan Shingon-shū|Kōyasan Shingon-shū]] | Main deity = {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦如来|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni|qq=}} | Founded = [[:en:Taiho|Taihō era]] (701–704 AD) | Founder = [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]] | Official name = Zōzusan Fumon-in Matsuo-ji (象頭山 普門院 松尾寺) | Pilgrimage = {{ill|Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|ja|新四国曼荼羅霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|qq=}} No.16 }} '''Matsuo-ji''' (松尾寺) is a [[:en:Kōyasan Shingon-shū|Kōyasan Shingon-shū]] temple located in [[:en:Kotohira, Kagawa|Kotohira, Kagawa]], [[:en:Nakatado District, Kagawa|Nakatado District, Kagawa]], [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]]. The temple's mountain name (山号) is Zōzusan (象頭山), and its cloister name (院号) is Fumon-in (普門院). The principal deity is {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦如来|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni|qq=}}, and it is the 16th temple in the {{ill|Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|ja|新四国曼荼羅霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|qq=}}. '''Temple hymn (Goeika, ご詠歌):''' *"Miagureba / kokoro mo kiyoki / Zōzusan / kin no gohei ni / kami zo masumasu"* (*"Looking up, / even the heart is purified, / Zōzusan, / with golden gohei, / the deity is ever-present."*) == History == [[File:Matuoji 20191125 02.jpg|thumb|180px|Sanmon (temple gate)]] According to the temple's legend, Matsuo-ji was founded in the [[:en:Taiho|Taihō era]] (701–704 AD) when [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]], the founder of [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendō]], climbed Mount Zōzusan. It is said that he received a divine revelation from {{ill|Kumbhīra (mythology)|ja|マカラ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumbhīra (mythology)|qq=}}, a protective deity (護法善神) dwelling in [[:en:Vulture Peak|Vulture Peak]] in India, which later became associated with [[:en:Konpira Gongen|Konpira Gongen]].Short history of Matsuo-ji However, many historical records were lost during the invasion of [[:en:Chōsokabe Motochika|Chōsokabe Motochika]] into [[:en:Sanuki Province|Sanuki Province]]. The earliest records of Konpira Gongen appear only in the early modern period. Before that, Matsuo-ji had its main hall at the current site of Hongū (本宮), enshrining an Eleven-Headed Kannon (十一面観音菩薩) as its principal deity. A shrine to the Thirty Guardian Deities (三十番神) was also located nearby. During the late 16th century, Matsuo-ji underwent significant transformations. A monk named Yūga (宥雅), a disciple of [[:en:Zentsū-ji|Zentsū-ji]], became the temple's abbot and founded Konkō-in (金光院). In 1573, he established a hall for Konpira Gongen (金毘羅堂) at the foot of the temple complex, separate from the original temple structure. In 1579, when [[:en:Chōsokabe Motochika|Chōsokabe Motochika]] sought to control religious institutions in Sanuki, Yūga fled, and the Chōsokabe forces appointed the monk Yūgen (宥厳) as the new abbot. During the Edo period, Konpira Gongen's faith expanded, and Matsuo-ji became a prominent center of pilgrimage. In 1813, the temple's Yakushi Hall (薬師堂) was rebuilt into a grand structure, housing a [[:en:Bhaisajyaguru|Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha]] statue and the Twelve Divine Generals (薬師十二神将). The temple flourished under the patronage of various regional rulers, and Konpira shrines spread throughout Japan. === Meiji Restoration and the Separation of Shinto and Buddhism === During the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) policies led to significant changes at Matsuo-ji. The temple complex was restructured: - The mountain name was changed from Zōzusan (象頭山) to Kotohirayama (琴平山). - The temple village was renamed from Konpira Village (金毘羅村) to Kotohira Village (琴平村). - The shrine was renamed from Konpira Gongen (金毘羅大権現) to [[:en:Kotohira-gū|Kotohira-gū]]. The temple itself was largely dismantled, with only Fumon-in (普門院) remaining under the name Matsuo-ji. Many Buddhist structures were converted into Shinto shrines or destroyed. However, some Buddhist artifacts, such as a Kamakura-period statue of {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦如来|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni|qq=}}, were preserved and enshrined in the temple. == Temple Grounds == [[File:Matuojo 20191125 01.jpg|thumb|180px|Main Gate (Sanmon)]] The temple complex includes the following structures: * Sanmon (temple gate) * Main Hall (本堂) * Bell Tower (鐘楼堂) * Kuri (庫裡, temple living quarters) == Cultural Properties == '''Kagawa Prefecture Designated Cultural Properties''' * '''Seated Statue of Kōbō Daishi''' (木造弘法大師座像) – Created in 1319 (Bunpō 3), made of cypress wood (檜材), designated on March 31, 2009. * '''Wooden Five-Ring Pagoda''' (木造五輪塔) – Contains an inscription dated 1604 (Keichō 9), associated with the monk Yūsei (宥盛), designated on March 31, 2009. == location == * '''Address:''' 973 Kawanishi, Kotohira, Nakatado District, Kagawa Prefecture, Japan (香川県仲多度郡琴平町字川西973番地) == Pilgrimage == * '''Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage''' ''' 15: {{ill|Kenshō-ji (Marugame)|ja|顕正寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenshō-ji (Marugame)|qq=}} → '''16: Matsuo-ji''' → 17: {{ill|Butsumo-in|ja|仏母院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Butsumo-in|qq=}} == References == === Notes === {{Notelist}} === Sources === {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[:en:Konpira Gongen|Konpira Gongen]] == External Links == * [http://www.mandala88.com/ Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage Website] {{translated page|ja|松尾寺 (香川県琴平町)|version=98510282|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Buddhist temples in Kagawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Kōyasan Shingon temples]] [[Category:En no Gyōja]] [[Category:Shinbutsu-shūgō]] [[Category:Konpira Shrine|Matsuo-ji]] [[Category:Kotohira, Kagawa]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Kotohira shrines]] }} [[zh:松尾寺 (琴平町)]] [[en:Matsuo-ji]] [[ja:%E6%9D%BE%E5%B0%BE%E5%AF%BA (%E9%A6%99%E5%B7%9D%E7%9C%8C%E7%90%B4%E5%B9%B3%E7%94%BA)]] [[ja:&action=edit]] [[sn:Matsuo-ji]] [[Category:Bettoji Temples]] [[Category:New Shikoku Mandala Sacred Sites]] [[Category:Buildings in Kotohira Town]] [[Category:History of Kotohira Town]]

revision.comment: Bot: classify by valid enwiki interwikis
revision.text:
{{Japanese temple infobox | Name = Matsuo-ji (松尾寺) | image = [[File:Matuozi02.jpg|250px]]
Main Hall | location = [[:en:Kotohira, Kagawa|Kotohira, Kagawa]], [[:en:Nakatado District, Kagawa|Nakatado District, Kagawa]], [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{Coord|34|11|8.8|N|133|49|6.2|E}} | Mount name = Zōzusan (象頭山) | Sect = [[:en:Kōyasan Shingon-shū|Kōyasan Shingon-shū]] | Main deity = {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦如来|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni|qq=}} | Founded = [[:en:Taiho|Taihō era]] (701–704 AD) | Founder = [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]] | Official name = Zōzusan Fumon-in Matsuo-ji (象頭山 普門院 松尾寺) | Pilgrimage = {{ill|Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|ja|新四国曼荼羅霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|qq=}} No.16 }} '''Matsuo-ji''' (松尾寺) is a [[:en:Kōyasan Shingon-shū|Kōyasan Shingon-shū]] temple located in [[:en:Kotohira, Kagawa|Kotohira, Kagawa]], [[:en:Nakatado District, Kagawa|Nakatado District, Kagawa]], [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]]. The temple's mountain name (山号) is Zōzusan (象頭山), and its cloister name (院号) is Fumon-in (普門院). The principal deity is {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦如来|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni|qq=}}, and it is the 16th temple in the {{ill|Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|ja|新四国曼荼羅霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|qq=}}. '''Temple hymn (Goeika, ご詠歌):''' *"Miagureba / kokoro mo kiyoki / Zōzusan / kin no gohei ni / kami zo masumasu"* (*"Looking up, / even the heart is purified, / Zōzusan, / with golden gohei, / the deity is ever-present."*) == History == [[File:Matuoji 20191125 02.jpg|thumb|180px|Sanmon (temple gate)]] According to the temple's legend, Matsuo-ji was founded in the [[:en:Taiho|Taihō era]] (701–704 AD) when [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]], the founder of [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendō]], climbed Mount Zōzusan. It is said that he received a divine revelation from {{ill|Kumbhīra (mythology)|ja|マカラ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumbhīra (mythology)|qq=}}, a protective deity (護法善神) dwelling in [[:en:Vulture Peak|Vulture Peak]] in India, which later became associated with [[:en:Konpira Gongen|Konpira Gongen]].Short history of Matsuo-ji However, many historical records were lost during the invasion of [[:en:Chōsokabe Motochika|Chōsokabe Motochika]] into [[:en:Sanuki Province|Sanuki Province]]. The earliest records of Konpira Gongen appear only in the early modern period. Before that, Matsuo-ji had its main hall at the current site of Hongū (本宮), enshrining an Eleven-Headed Kannon (十一面観音菩薩) as its principal deity. A shrine to the Thirty Guardian Deities (三十番神) was also located nearby. During the late 16th century, Matsuo-ji underwent significant transformations. A monk named Yūga (宥雅), a disciple of [[:en:Zentsū-ji|Zentsū-ji]], became the temple's abbot and founded Konkō-in (金光院). In 1573, he established a hall for Konpira Gongen (金毘羅堂) at the foot of the temple complex, separate from the original temple structure. In 1579, when [[:en:Chōsokabe Motochika|Chōsokabe Motochika]] sought to control religious institutions in Sanuki, Yūga fled, and the Chōsokabe forces appointed the monk Yūgen (宥厳) as the new abbot. During the Edo period, Konpira Gongen's faith expanded, and Matsuo-ji became a prominent center of pilgrimage. In 1813, the temple's Yakushi Hall (薬師堂) was rebuilt into a grand structure, housing a [[:en:Bhaisajyaguru|Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha]] statue and the Twelve Divine Generals (薬師十二神将). The temple flourished under the patronage of various regional rulers, and Konpira shrines spread throughout Japan. === Meiji Restoration and the Separation of Shinto and Buddhism === During the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) policies led to significant changes at Matsuo-ji. The temple complex was restructured: - The mountain name was changed from Zōzusan (象頭山) to Kotohirayama (琴平山). - The temple village was renamed from Konpira Village (金毘羅村) to Kotohira Village (琴平村). - The shrine was renamed from Konpira Gongen (金毘羅大権現) to [[:en:Kotohira-gū|Kotohira-gū]]. The temple itself was largely dismantled, with only Fumon-in (普門院) remaining under the name Matsuo-ji. Many Buddhist structures were converted into Shinto shrines or destroyed. However, some Buddhist artifacts, such as a Kamakura-period statue of {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦如来|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni|qq=}}, were preserved and enshrined in the temple. == Temple Grounds == [[File:Matuojo 20191125 01.jpg|thumb|180px|Main Gate (Sanmon)]] The temple complex includes the following structures: * Sanmon (temple gate) * Main Hall (本堂) * Bell Tower (鐘楼堂) * Kuri (庫裡, temple living quarters) == Cultural Properties == '''Kagawa Prefecture Designated Cultural Properties''' * '''Seated Statue of Kōbō Daishi''' (木造弘法大師座像) – Created in 1319 (Bunpō 3), made of cypress wood (檜材), designated on March 31, 2009. * '''Wooden Five-Ring Pagoda''' (木造五輪塔) – Contains an inscription dated 1604 (Keichō 9), associated with the monk Yūsei (宥盛), designated on March 31, 2009. == location == * '''Address:''' 973 Kawanishi, Kotohira, Nakatado District, Kagawa Prefecture, Japan (香川県仲多度郡琴平町字川西973番地) == Pilgrimage == * '''Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage''' ''' 15: {{ill|Kenshō-ji (Marugame)|ja|顕正寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenshō-ji (Marugame)|qq=}} → '''16: Matsuo-ji''' → 17: {{ill|Butsumo-in|ja|仏母院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Butsumo-in|qq=}} == References == === Notes === {{Notelist}} === Sources === {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[:en:Konpira Gongen|Konpira Gongen]] == External Links == * [http://www.mandala88.com/ Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage Website] {{translated page|ja|松尾寺 (香川県琴平町)|version=98510282|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Buddhist temples in Kagawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Kōyasan Shingon temples]] [[Category:En no Gyōja]] [[Category:Shinbutsu-shūgō]] [[Category:Konpira Shrine|Matsuo-ji]] [[Category:Kotohira, Kagawa]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Kotohira shrines]] }} [[zh:松尾寺 (琴平町)]] [[en:Matsuo-ji]] [[ja:%E6%9D%BE%E5%B0%BE%E5%AF%BA (%E9%A6%99%E5%B7%9D%E7%9C%8C%E7%90%B4%E5%B9%B3%E7%94%BA)]] [[ja:&action=edit]] [[sn:Matsuo-ji]] [[Category:Bettoji Temples]] [[Category:New Shikoku Mandala Sacred Sites]] [[Category:Buildings in Kotohira Town]] [[Category:History of Kotohira Town]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] {{Commons category|Matsuo-ji (Kotohira)}} [[ja:松尾寺 (香川県琴平町)]] {{Commons category|Matsuo-ji (Kotohira)}} [[ja:松尾寺 (香川県琴平町)]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]]

{{Japanese temple infobox | Name = Matsuo-ji (松尾寺) | image = [[File:Matuozi02.jpg|250px]]
Main Hall | location = [[:en:Kotohira, Kagawa|Kotohira, Kagawa]], [[:en:Nakatado District, Kagawa|Nakatado District, Kagawa]], [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{Coord|34|11|8.8|N|133|49|6.2|E}} | Mount name = Zōzusan (象頭山) | Sect = [[:en:Kōyasan Shingon-shū|Kōyasan Shingon-shū]] | Main deity = {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦如来|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni|qq=}} | Founded = [[:en:Taiho|Taihō era]] (701–704 AD) | Founder = [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]] | Official name = Zōzusan Fumon-in Matsuo-ji (象頭山 普門院 松尾寺) | Pilgrimage = {{ill|Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|ja|新四国曼荼羅霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|qq=}} No.16 }} '''Matsuo-ji''' (松尾寺) is a [[:en:Kōyasan Shingon-shū|Kōyasan Shingon-shū]] temple located in [[:en:Kotohira, Kagawa|Kotohira, Kagawa]], [[:en:Nakatado District, Kagawa|Nakatado District, Kagawa]], [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]]. The temple's mountain name (山号) is Zōzusan (象頭山), and its cloister name (院号) is Fumon-in (普門院). The principal deity is {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦如来|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni|qq=}}, and it is the 16th temple in the {{ill|Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|ja|新四国曼荼羅霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|qq=}}. '''Temple hymn (Goeika, ご詠歌):''' *"Miagureba / kokoro mo kiyoki / Zōzusan / kin no gohei ni / kami zo masumasu"* (*"Looking up, / even the heart is purified, / Zōzusan, / with golden gohei, / the deity is ever-present."*) == History == [[File:Matuoji 20191125 02.jpg|thumb|180px|Sanmon (temple gate)]] According to the temple's legend, Matsuo-ji was founded in the [[:en:Taiho|Taihō era]] (701–704 AD) when [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]], the founder of [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendō]], climbed Mount Zōzusan. It is said that he received a divine revelation from {{ill|Kumbhīra (mythology)|ja|マカラ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumbhīra (mythology)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to マカラ (神話)}}, a protective deity (護法善神) dwelling in [[:en:Vulture Peak|Vulture Peak]] in India, which later became associated with [[:en:Konpira Gongen|Konpira Gongen]].Short history of Matsuo-ji However, many historical records were lost during the invasion of [[:en:Chōsokabe Motochika|Chōsokabe Motochika]] into [[:en:Sanuki Province|Sanuki Province]]. The earliest records of Konpira Gongen appear only in the early modern period. Before that, Matsuo-ji had its main hall at the current site of Hongū (本宮), enshrining an Eleven-Headed Kannon (十一面観音菩薩) as its principal deity. A shrine to the Thirty Guardian Deities (三十番神) was also located nearby. During the late 16th century, Matsuo-ji underwent significant transformations. A monk named Yūga (宥雅), a disciple of [[:en:Zentsū-ji|Zentsū-ji]], became the temple's abbot and founded Konkō-in (金光院). In 1573, he established a hall for Konpira Gongen (金毘羅堂) at the foot of the temple complex, separate from the original temple structure. In 1579, when [[:en:Chōsokabe Motochika|Chōsokabe Motochika]] sought to control religious institutions in Sanuki, Yūga fled, and the Chōsokabe forces appointed the monk Yūgen (宥厳) as the new abbot. During the Edo period, Konpira Gongen's faith expanded, and Matsuo-ji became a prominent center of pilgrimage. In 1813, the temple's Yakushi Hall (薬師堂) was rebuilt into a grand structure, housing a [[:en:Bhaisajyaguru|Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha]] statue and the Twelve Divine Generals (薬師十二神将). The temple flourished under the patronage of various regional rulers, and Konpira shrines spread throughout Japan. === Meiji Restoration and the Separation of Shinto and Buddhism === During the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) policies led to significant changes at Matsuo-ji. The temple complex was restructured: - The mountain name was changed from Zōzusan (象頭山) to Kotohirayama (琴平山). - The temple village was renamed from Konpira Village (金毘羅村) to Kotohira Village (琴平村). - The shrine was renamed from Konpira Gongen (金毘羅大権現) to [[:en:Kotohira-gū|Kotohira-gū]]. The temple itself was largely dismantled, with only Fumon-in (普門院) remaining under the name Matsuo-ji. Many Buddhist structures were converted into Shinto shrines or destroyed. However, some Buddhist artifacts, such as a Kamakura-period statue of {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦如来|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni|qq=}}, were preserved and enshrined in the temple. == Temple Grounds == [[File:Matuojo 20191125 01.jpg|thumb|180px|Main Gate (Sanmon)]] The temple complex includes the following structures: * Sanmon (temple gate) * Main Hall (本堂) * Bell Tower (鐘楼堂) * Kuri (庫裡, temple living quarters) == Cultural Properties == '''Kagawa Prefecture Designated Cultural Properties''' * '''Seated Statue of Kōbō Daishi''' (木造弘法大師座像) – Created in 1319 (Bunpō 3), made of cypress wood (檜材), designated on March 31, 2009. * '''Wooden Five-Ring Pagoda''' (木造五輪塔) – Contains an inscription dated 1604 (Keichō 9), associated with the monk Yūsei (宥盛), designated on March 31, 2009. == location == * '''Address:''' 973 Kawanishi, Kotohira, Nakatado District, Kagawa Prefecture, Japan (香川県仲多度郡琴平町字川西973番地) == Pilgrimage == * '''Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage''' ''' 15: {{ill|Kenshō-ji (Marugame)|ja|顕正寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenshō-ji (Marugame)|qq=}} → '''16: Matsuo-ji''' → 17: {{ill|Butsumo-in|ja|仏母院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Butsumo-in|qq=}} == References == === Notes === {{Notelist}} === Sources === {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[:en:Konpira Gongen|Konpira Gongen]] == External Links == * [http://www.mandala88.com/ Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage Website] {{translated page|ja|松尾寺 (香川県琴平町)|version=98510282|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Kotohira shrines]] }} [[zh:松尾寺 (琴平町)]] [[en:Matsuo-ji]] [[ja:%E6%9D%BE%E5%B0%BE%E5%AF%BA (%E9%A6%99%E5%B7%9D%E7%9C%8C%E7%90%B4%E5%B9%B3%E7%94%BA)]] revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Japanese temple infobox | Name = Matsuo-ji (松尾寺) | image = [[File:Matuozi02.jpg|250px]]
Main Hall | location = [[:en:Kotohira, Kagawa|Kotohira, Kagawa]], [[:en:Nakatado District, Kagawa|Nakatado District, Kagawa]], [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{Coord|34|11|8.8|N|133|49|6.2|E}} | Mount name = Zōzusan (象頭山) | Sect = [[:en:Kōyasan Shingon-shū|Kōyasan Shingon-shū]] | Main deity = {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}} | Founded = [[:en:Taiho|Taihō era]] (701–704 AD) | Founder = [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]] | Official name = Zōzusan Fumon-in Matsuo-ji (象頭山 普門院 松尾寺) | Pilgrimage = {{ill|Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|ja|新四国曼荼羅霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|qq=}} No.16 }} '''Matsuo-ji''' (松尾寺) is a [[:en:Kōyasan Shingon-shū|Kōyasan Shingon-shū]] temple located in [[:en:Kotohira, Kagawa|Kotohira, Kagawa]], [[:en:Nakatado District, Kagawa|Nakatado District, Kagawa]], [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]]. The temple's mountain name (山号) is Zōzusan (象頭山), and its cloister name (院号) is Fumon-in (普門院). The principal deity is {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}}, and it is the 16th temple in the {{ill|Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|ja|新四国曼荼羅霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|qq=}}. '''Temple hymn (Goeika, ご詠歌):''' *"Miagureba / kokoro mo kiyoki / Zōzusan / kin no gohei ni / kami zo masumasu"* (*"Looking up, / even the heart is purified, / Zōzusan, / with golden gohei, / the deity is ever-present."*) == History == [[File:Matuoji 20191125 02.jpg|thumb|180px|Sanmon (temple gate)]] According to the temple's legend, Matsuo-ji was founded in the [[:en:Taiho|Taihō era]] (701–704 AD) when [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]], the founder of [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendō]], climbed Mount Zōzusan. It is said that he received a divine revelation from {{ill|Kumbhīra (mythology)|ja|マカラ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumbhīra (mythology)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to マカラ (神話)}}, a protective deity (護法善神) dwelling in [[:en:Vulture Peak|Vulture Peak]] in India, which later became associated with [[:en:Konpira Gongen|Konpira Gongen]].Short history of Matsuo-ji However, many historical records were lost during the invasion of [[:en:Chōsokabe Motochika|Chōsokabe Motochika]] into [[:en:Sanuki Province|Sanuki Province]]. The earliest records of Konpira Gongen appear only in the early modern period. Before that, Matsuo-ji had its main hall at the current site of Hongū (本宮), enshrining an Eleven-Headed Kannon (十一面観音菩薩) as its principal deity. A shrine to the Thirty Guardian Deities (三十番神) was also located nearby. During the late 16th century, Matsuo-ji underwent significant transformations. A monk named Yūga (宥雅), a disciple of [[:en:Zentsū-ji|Zentsū-ji]], became the temple's abbot and founded Konkō-in (金光院). In 1573, he established a hall for Konpira Gongen (金毘羅堂) at the foot of the temple complex, separate from the original temple structure. In 1579, when [[:en:Chōsokabe Motochika|Chōsokabe Motochika]] sought to control religious institutions in Sanuki, Yūga fled, and the Chōsokabe forces appointed the monk Yūgen (宥厳) as the new abbot. During the Edo period, Konpira Gongen's faith expanded, and Matsuo-ji became a prominent center of pilgrimage. In 1813, the temple's Yakushi Hall (薬師堂) was rebuilt into a grand structure, housing a [[:en:Bhaisajyaguru|Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha]] statue and the Twelve Divine Generals (薬師十二神将). The temple flourished under the patronage of various regional rulers, and Konpira shrines spread throughout Japan. === Meiji Restoration and the Separation of Shinto and Buddhism === During the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) policies led to significant changes at Matsuo-ji. The temple complex was restructured: - The mountain name was changed from Zōzusan (象頭山) to Kotohirayama (琴平山). - The temple village was renamed from Konpira Village (金毘羅村) to Kotohira Village (琴平村). - The shrine was renamed from Konpira Gongen (金毘羅大権現) to [[:en:Kotohira-gū|Kotohira-gū]]. The temple itself was largely dismantled, with only Fumon-in (普門院) remaining under the name Matsuo-ji. Many Buddhist structures were converted into Shinto shrines or destroyed. However, some Buddhist artifacts, such as a Kamakura-period statue of {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}}, were preserved and enshrined in the temple. == Temple Grounds == [[File:Matuojo 20191125 01.jpg|thumb|180px|Main Gate (Sanmon)]] The temple complex includes the following structures: * Sanmon (temple gate) * Main Hall (本堂) * Bell Tower (鐘楼堂) * Kuri (庫裡, temple living quarters) == Cultural Properties == '''Kagawa Prefecture Designated Cultural Properties''' * '''Seated Statue of Kōbō Daishi''' (木造弘法大師座像) – Created in 1319 (Bunpō 3), made of cypress wood (檜材), designated on March 31, 2009. * '''Wooden Five-Ring Pagoda''' (木造五輪塔) – Contains an inscription dated 1604 (Keichō 9), associated with the monk Yūsei (宥盛), designated on March 31, 2009. == location == * '''Address:''' 973 Kawanishi, Kotohira, Nakatado District, Kagawa Prefecture, Japan (香川県仲多度郡琴平町字川西973番地) == Pilgrimage == * '''Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage''' ''' 15: {{ill|Kenshō-ji (Marugame)|ja|顕正寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenshō-ji (Marugame)|qq=}} → '''16: Matsuo-ji''' → 17: {{ill|Butsumo-in|ja|仏母院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Butsumo-in|qq=}} == References == === Notes === {{Notelist}} === Sources === {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[:en:Konpira Gongen|Konpira Gongen]] == External Links == * [http://www.mandala88.com/ Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage Website] {{translated page|ja|松尾寺 (香川県琴平町)|version=98510282|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Buddhist temples in Kagawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Kōyasan Shingon temples]] [[Category:En no Gyōja]] [[Category:Kotohira, Kagawa]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Kotohira shrines]] }} [[zh:松尾寺 (琴平町)]] [[en:Matsuo-ji]] [[ja:%E6%9D%BE%E5%B0%BE%E5%AF%BA (%E9%A6%99%E5%B7%9D%E7%9C%8C%E7%90%B4%E5%B9%B3%E7%94%BA)]] [[ja:&action=edit]] [[sn:Matsuo-ji]] [[Category:Bettoji Temples]] [[Category:New Shikoku Mandala Sacred Sites]] [[Category:Buildings in Kotohira Town]] [[Category:History of Kotohira Town]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] {{Commons category|Matsuo-ji (Kotohira)}} [[ja:松尾寺 (香川県琴平町)]] {{Commons category|Matsuo-ji (Kotohira)}} [[ja:松尾寺 (香川県琴平町)]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en However, some Buddhist artifacts, such as a Kamakura-period statue of {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}}, were preserved and enshrined in the temple. == Temple Grounds == [[File:Matuojo 20191125 01.jpg|thumb|180px|Main Gate (Sanmon)]] The temple complex includes the following structures: * Sanmon (temple gate) * Main Hall (本堂) * Bell Tower (鐘楼堂) * Kuri (庫裡, temple living quarters) == Cultural Properties == '''Kagawa Prefecture Designated Cultural Properties''' * '''Seated Statue of Kōbō Daishi''' (木造弘法大師座像) – Created in 1319 (Bunpō 3), made of cypress wood (檜材), designated on March 31, 2009. * '''Wooden Five-Ring Pagoda''' (木造五輪塔) – Contains an inscription dated 1604 (Keichō 9), associated with the monk Yūsei (宥盛), designated on March 31, 2009. == location == * '''Address:''' 973 Kawanishi, Kotohira, Nakatado District, Kagawa Prefecture, Japan (香川県仲多度郡琴平町字川西973番地) == Pilgrimage == * '''Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage''' ''' 15: {{ill|Kenshō-ji (Marugame)|ja|顕正寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenshō-ji (Marugame)|qq=}} → '''16: Matsuo-ji''' → 17: {{ill|Butsumo-in|ja|仏母院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Butsumo-in|qq=}} == References == === Notes === {{Notelist}} === Sources === {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[:en:Konpira Gongen|Konpira Gongen]] == External Links == * [http://www.mandala88.com/ Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage Website] {{translated page|ja|松尾寺 (香川県琴平町)|version=98510282|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Japanese temple infobox | Name = Matsuo-ji (松尾寺) | image = [[File:Matuozi02.jpg|250px]]
Main Hall | location = [[:en:Kotohira, Kagawa|Kotohira, Kagawa]], [[:en:Nakatado District, Kagawa|Nakatado District, Kagawa]], [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{Coord|34|11|8.8|N|133|49|6.2|E}} | Mount name = Zōzusan (象頭山) | Sect = [[:en:Kōyasan Shingon-shū|Kōyasan Shingon-shū]] | Main deity = {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}} | Founded = [[:en:Taiho|Taihō era]] (701–704 AD) | Founder = [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]] | Official name = Zōzusan Fumon-in Matsuo-ji (象頭山 普門院 松尾寺) | Pilgrimage = {{ill|Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|ja|新四国曼荼羅霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|qq=}} No.16 }} '''Matsuo-ji''' (松尾寺) is a [[:en:Kōyasan Shingon-shū|Kōyasan Shingon-shū]] temple located in [[:en:Kotohira, Kagawa|Kotohira, Kagawa]], [[:en:Nakatado District, Kagawa|Nakatado District, Kagawa]], [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]]. The temple's mountain name (山号) is Zōzusan (象頭山), and its cloister name (院号) is Fumon-in (普門院). The principal deity is {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}}, and it is the 16th temple in the {{ill|Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|ja|新四国曼荼羅霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|qq=}}. '''Temple hymn (Goeika, ご詠歌):''' *"Miagureba / kokoro mo kiyoki / Zōzusan / kin no gohei ni / kami zo masumasu"* (*"Looking up, / even the heart is purified, / Zōzusan, / with golden gohei, / the deity is ever-present."*) == History == [[File:Matuoji 20191125 02.jpg|thumb|180px|Sanmon (temple gate)]] According to the temple's legend, Matsuo-ji was founded in the [[:en:Taiho|Taihō era]] (701–704 AD) when [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]], the founder of [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendō]], climbed Mount Zōzusan. It is said that he received a divine revelation from {{ill|Kumbhīra (mythology)|ja|マカラ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumbhīra (mythology)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to マカラ (神話)}}, a protective deity (護法善神) dwelling in [[:en:Vulture Peak|Vulture Peak]] in India, which later became associated with [[:en:Konpira Gongen|Konpira Gongen]].Short history of Matsuo-ji However, many historical records were lost during the invasion of [[:en:Chōsokabe Motochika|Chōsokabe Motochika]] into [[:en:Sanuki Province|Sanuki Province]]. The earliest records of Konpira Gongen appear only in the early modern period. Before that, Matsuo-ji had its main hall at the current site of Hongū (本宮), enshrining an Eleven-Headed Kannon (十一面観音菩薩) as its principal deity. A shrine to the Thirty Guardian Deities (三十番神) was also located nearby. During the late 16th century, Matsuo-ji underwent significant transformations. A monk named Yūga (宥雅), a disciple of [[:en:Zentsū-ji|Zentsū-ji]], became the temple's abbot and founded Konkō-in (金光院). In 1573, he established a hall for Konpira Gongen (金毘羅堂) at the foot of the temple complex, separate from the original temple structure. In 1579, when [[:en:Chōsokabe Motochika|Chōsokabe Motochika]] sought to control religious institutions in Sanuki, Yūga fled, and the Chōsokabe forces appointed the monk Yūgen (宥厳) as the new abbot. During the Edo period, Konpira Gongen's faith expanded, and Matsuo-ji became a prominent center of pilgrimage. In 1813, the temple's Yakushi Hall (薬師堂) was rebuilt into a grand structure, housing a [[:en:Bhaisajyaguru|Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha]] statue and the Twelve Divine Generals (薬師十二神将). The temple flourished under the patronage of various regional rulers, and Konpira shrines spread throughout Japan. === Meiji Restoration and the Separation of Shinto and Buddhism === During the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) policies led to significant changes at Matsuo-ji. The temple complex was restructured: - The mountain name was changed from Zōzusan (象頭山) to Kotohirayama (琴平山). - The temple village was renamed from Konpira Village (金毘羅村) to Kotohira Village (琴平村). - The shrine was renamed from Konpira Gongen (金毘羅大権現) to [[:en:Kotohira-gū|Kotohira-gū]]. The temple itself was largely dismantled, with only Fumon-in (普門院) remaining under the name Matsuo-ji. Many Buddhist structures were converted into Shinto shrines or destroyed. However, some Buddhist artifacts, such as a Kamakura-period statue of {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}}, were preserved and enshrined in the temple. == Temple Grounds == [[File:Matuojo 20191125 01.jpg|thumb|180px|Main Gate (Sanmon)]] The temple complex includes the following structures: * Sanmon (temple gate) * Main Hall (本堂) * Bell Tower (鐘楼堂) * Kuri (庫裡, temple living quarters) == Cultural Properties == '''Kagawa Prefecture Designated Cultural Properties''' * '''Seated Statue of Kōbō Daishi''' (木造弘法大師座像) – Created in 1319 (Bunpō 3), made of cypress wood (檜材), designated on March 31, 2009. * '''Wooden Five-Ring Pagoda''' (木造五輪塔) – Contains an inscription dated 1604 (Keichō 9), associated with the monk Yūsei (宥盛), designated on March 31, 2009. == location == * '''Address:''' 973 Kawanishi, Kotohira, Nakatado District, Kagawa Prefecture, Japan (香川県仲多度郡琴平町字川西973番地) == Pilgrimage == * '''Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage''' ''' 15: {{ill|Kenshō-ji (Marugame)|ja|顕正寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenshō-ji (Marugame)|qq=}} → '''16: Matsuo-ji''' → 17: {{ill|Butsumo-in|ja|仏母院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Butsumo-in|qq=}} == References == === Notes === {{Notelist}} === Sources === {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[:en:Konpira Gongen|Konpira Gongen]] == External Links == * [http://www.mandala88.com/ Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage Website] {{translated page|ja|松尾寺 (香川県琴平町)|version=98510282|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Kotohira shrines]] }} [[zh:松尾寺 (琴平町)]] [[en:Matsuo-ji]] [[sn:E6%9D%BE%E5%B0%BE%E5%AF%BA (%E9%A6%99%E5%B7%9D%E7%9C%8C%E7%90%B4%E5%B9%B3%E7%94%BA)]] Town]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] {{Commons category|Matsuo-ji (Kotohira)}} [[ja:松尾寺 (香川県琴平町)]] {{Commons category|Matsuo-ji (Kotohira)}} [[ja:松尾寺 (香川県琴平町)]] [[Category:translated pages with valid A shrine to the Thirty Guardian Deities (三十番神) was also located nearby. During the late 16th century, Matsuo-ji underwent significant transformations. A monk named Yūga (宥雅), a disciple of [[:en:Zentsū-ji|Zentsū-ji]], became the temple's abbot and founded Konkō-in (金光院). In 1573, he established a hall for Konpira Gongen (金毘羅堂) at the foot of the temple complex, separate from the original temple structure. In 1579, when [[:en:Chōsokabe Motochika|Chōsokabe Motochika]] sought to control religious institutions in Sanuki, Yūga fled, and the Chōsokabe forces appointed the monk Yūgen (宥厳) as the new abbot. During the Edo period, Konpira Gongen's faith expanded, and Matsuo-ji became a prominent center of pilgrimage. In 1813, the temple's Yakushi Hall (薬師堂) was rebuilt into a grand structure, housing a [[:en:Bhaisajyaguru|Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha]] statue and the Twelve Divine Generals (薬師十二神将). The temple flourished under the patronage of various regional rulers, and Konpira shrines spread throughout Japan. === Meiji Restoration and the Separation of Shinto and Buddhism === During the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) policies led to significant changes at Matsuo-ji. The temple complex was restructured: - The mountain name was changed from Zōzusan (象頭山) to Kotohirayama (琴平山). - The temple village was renamed from Konpira Village (金毘羅村) to Kotohira Village (琴平村). - The shrine was renamed from Konpira Gongen (金毘羅大権現) to [[:en:Kotohira-gū|Kotohira-gū]]. The temple itself was largely dismantled, with only Fumon-in (普門院) remaining under the name Matsuo-ji. Many Buddhist structures were converted into Shinto shrines or destroyed. However, some Buddhist artifacts, such as a Kamakura-period statue of {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}}, were preserved and enshrined in the temple. == Temple Grounds == [[File:Matuojo 20191125 01.jpg|thumb|180px|Main Gate (Sanmon)]] The temple complex includes the following structures: * Sanmon (temple gate) * Main Hall (本堂) * Bell Tower (鐘楼堂) * Kuri (庫裡, temple living quarters) == Cultural Properties == '''Kagawa Prefecture Designated Cultural Properties''' * '''Seated Statue of Kōbō Daishi''' (木造弘法大師座像) – Created in 1319 (Bunpō 3), made of cypress wood (檜材), designated on March 31, 2009. * '''Wooden Five-Ring Pagoda''' (木造五輪塔) – Contains an inscription dated 1604 (Keichō 9), associated with the monk Yūsei (宥盛), designated on March 31, 2009. == location == * '''Address:''' 973 Kawanishi, Kotohira, Nakatado District, Kagawa Prefecture, Japan (香川県仲多度郡琴平町字川西973番地) == Pilgrimage == * '''Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage''' ''' 15: {{ill|Kenshō-ji (Marugame)|ja|顕正寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenshō-ji (Marugame)|qq=}} → '''16: Matsuo-ji''' → 17: {{ill|Butsumo-in|ja|仏母院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Butsumo-in|qq=}} == References == === Notes === {{Notelist}} === Sources === {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[:en:Konpira Gongen|Konpira Gongen]] == External Links == * [http://www.mandala88.com/ Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage Website] {{translated page|ja|松尾寺 (香川県琴平町)|version=98510282|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11529610]] Wikidata name: Matsuo-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:20, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Japanese temple infobox | Name = Matsuo-ji (松尾寺) | image = [[File:Matuozi02.jpg|250px]]
Main Hall | location = [[:en:Kotohira, Kagawa|Kotohira, Kagawa]], [[:en:Nakatado District, Kagawa|Nakatado District, Kagawa]], [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{Coord|34|11|8.8|N|133|49|6.2|E}} | Mount name = Zōzusan (象頭山) | Sect = [[:en:Kōyasan Shingon-shū|Kōyasan Shingon-shū]] | Main deity = {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}} | Founded = [[:en:Taiho|Taihō era]] (701–704 AD) | Founder = [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]] | Official name = Zōzusan Fumon-in Matsuo-ji (象頭山 普門院 松尾寺) | Pilgrimage = {{ill|Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|ja|新四国曼荼羅霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|qq=}} No.16 }} '''Matsuo-ji''' (松尾寺) is a [[:en:Kōyasan Shingon-shū|Kōyasan Shingon-shū]] temple located in [[:en:Kotohira, Kagawa|Kotohira, Kagawa]], [[:en:Nakatado District, Kagawa|Nakatado District, Kagawa]], [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]]. The temple's mountain name (山号) is Zōzusan (象頭山), and its cloister name (院号) is Fumon-in (普門院). The principal deity is {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}}, and it is the 16th temple in the {{ill|Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|ja|新四国曼荼羅霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|qq=}}. '''Temple hymn (Goeika, ご詠歌):''' *"Miagureba / kokoro mo kiyoki / Zōzusan / kin no gohei ni / kami zo masumasu"* (*"Looking up, / even the heart is purified, / Zōzusan, / with golden gohei, / the deity is ever-present."*) == History == [[File:Matuoji 20191125 02.jpg|thumb|180px|Sanmon (temple gate)]] According to the temple's legend, Matsuo-ji was founded in the [[:en:Taiho|Taihō era]] (701–704 AD) when [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]], the founder of [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendō]], climbed Mount Zōzusan. It is said that he received a divine revelation from {{ill|Kumbhīra (mythology)|ja|マカラ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumbhīra (mythology)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to マカラ (神話)}}, a protective deity (護法善神) dwelling in [[:en:Vulture Peak|Vulture Peak]] in India, which later became associated with [[:en:Konpira Gongen|Konpira Gongen]].Short history of Matsuo-ji However, many historical records were lost during the invasion of [[:en:Chōsokabe Motochika|Chōsokabe Motochika]] into [[:en:Sanuki Province|Sanuki Province]]. The earliest records of Konpira Gongen appear only in the early modern period. Before that, Matsuo-ji had its main hall at the current site of Hongū (本宮), enshrining an Eleven-Headed Kannon (十一面観音菩薩) as its principal deity. A shrine to the Thirty Guardian Deities (三十番神) was also located nearby. During the late 16th century, Matsuo-ji underwent significant transformations. A monk named Yūga (宥雅), a disciple of [[:en:Zentsū-ji|Zentsū-ji]], became the temple's abbot and founded Konkō-in (金光院). In 1573, he established a hall for Konpira Gongen (金毘羅堂) at the foot of the temple complex, separate from the original temple structure. In 1579, when [[:en:Chōsokabe Motochika|Chōsokabe Motochika]] sought to control religious institutions in Sanuki, Yūga fled, and the Chōsokabe forces appointed the monk Yūgen (宥厳) as the new abbot. During the Edo period, Konpira Gongen's faith expanded, and Matsuo-ji became a prominent center of pilgrimage. In 1813, the temple's Yakushi Hall (薬師堂) was rebuilt into a grand structure, housing a [[:en:Bhaisajyaguru|Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha]] statue and the Twelve Divine Generals (薬師十二神将). The temple flourished under the patronage of various regional rulers, and Konpira shrines spread throughout Japan. === Meiji Restoration and the Separation of Shinto and Buddhism === During the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) policies led to significant changes at Matsuo-ji. The temple complex was restructured: - The mountain name was changed from Zōzusan (象頭山) to Kotohirayama (琴平山). - The temple village was renamed from Konpira Village (金毘羅村) to Kotohira Village (琴平村). - The shrine was renamed from Konpira Gongen (金毘羅大権現) to [[:en:Kotohira-gū|Kotohira-gū]]. The temple itself was largely dismantled, with only Fumon-in (普門院) remaining under the name Matsuo-ji. Many Buddhist structures were converted into Shinto shrines or destroyed. However, some Buddhist artifacts, such as a Kamakura-period statue of {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}}, were preserved and enshrined in the temple. == Temple Grounds == [[File:Matuojo 20191125 01.jpg|thumb|180px|Main Gate (Sanmon)]] The temple complex includes the following structures: * Sanmon (temple gate) * Main Hall (本堂) * Bell Tower (鐘楼堂) * Kuri (庫裡, temple living quarters) == Cultural Properties == '''Kagawa Prefecture Designated Cultural Properties''' * '''Seated Statue of Kōbō Daishi''' (木造弘法大師座像) – Created in 1319 (Bunpō 3), made of cypress wood (檜材), designated on March 31, 2009. * '''Wooden Five-Ring Pagoda''' (木造五輪塔) – Contains an inscription dated 1604 (Keichō 9), associated with the monk Yūsei (宥盛), designated on March 31, 2009. == location == * '''Address:''' 973 Kawanishi, Kotohira, Nakatado District, Kagawa Prefecture, Japan (香川県仲多度郡琴平町字川西973番地) == Pilgrimage == * '''Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage''' ''' 15: {{ill|Kenshō-ji (Marugame)|ja|顕正寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenshō-ji (Marugame)|qq=}} → '''16: Matsuo-ji''' → 17: Gongen|Konpira Gongen]] == External Links == * [http://www.mandala88.com/ Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage Website] {{translated page|ja|松尾寺 (香川県琴平町)|version=98510282|comment=Imported full ja history}}
