{{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Matuozi02.jpg|religious_affiliation={{ill|Kōyasan Shingon-shū|qid=Q3077357}}|prefecture={{ill|Kotohira|qid=Q1271483}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.185778|N|133.818389|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|deity={{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qid=Q11644537}}|part_of={{ill|Shin-Shikoku Mandara Pilgrimage|qid=Q11501519}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11529610]] Wikidata name: Matsuo-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:20, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Japanese temple infobox | Name = Matsuo-ji (松尾寺) | image = [[File:Matuozi02.jpg|250px]]
Main Hall | location = [[:en:Kotohira, Kagawa|Kotohira, Kagawa]], [[:en:Nakatado District, Kagawa|Nakatado District, Kagawa]], [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{Coord|34|11|8.8|N|133|49|6.2|E}} | Mount name = Zōzusan (象頭山) | Sect = [[:en:Kōyasan Shingon-shū|Kōyasan Shingon-shū]] | Main deity = {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}} | Founded = [[:en:Taiho|Taihō era]] (701–704 AD) | Founder = [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]] | Official name = Zōzusan Fumon-in Matsuo-ji (象頭山 普門院 松尾寺) | Pilgrimage = {{ill|Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|ja|新四国曼荼羅霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|qq=|1=Shin-Shikoku Mandara Pilgrimage|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11501519}} No.16 }} '''Matsuo-ji''' (松尾寺) is a [[:en:Kōyasan Shingon-shū|Kōyasan Shingon-shū]] temple located in [[:en:Kotohira, Kagawa|Kotohira, Kagawa]], [[:en:Nakatado District, Kagawa|Nakatado District, Kagawa]], [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]]. The temple's mountain name (山号) is Zōzusan (象頭山), and its cloister name (院号) is Fumon-in (普門院). The principal deity is {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}}, and it is the 16th temple in the {{ill|Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|ja|新四国曼荼羅霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|qq=|1=Shin-Shikoku Mandara Pilgrimage|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11501519}}. '''Temple hymn (Goeika, ご詠歌):''' *"Miagureba / kokoro mo kiyoki / Zōzusan / kin no gohei ni / kami zo masumasu"* (*"Looking up, / even the heart is purified, / Zōzusan, / with golden gohei, / the deity is ever-present."*) == History == [[File:Matuoji 20191125 02.jpg|thumb|180px|Sanmon (temple gate)]] According to the temple's legend, Matsuo-ji was founded in the [[:en:Taiho|Taihō era]] (701–704 AD) when [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]], the founder of [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendō]], climbed Mount Zōzusan. It is said that he received a divine revelation from {{ill|Kumbhīra (mythology)|ja|マカラ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumbhīra (mythology)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to マカラ (神話)|1=Makara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1136842}}, a protective deity (護法善神) dwelling in [[:en:Vulture Peak|Vulture Peak]] in India, which later became associated with [[:en:Konpira Gongen|Konpira Gongen]].Short history of Matsuo-ji However, many historical records were lost during the invasion of [[:en:Chōsokabe Motochika|Chōsokabe Motochika]] into [[:en:Sanuki Province|Sanuki Province]]. The earliest records of Konpira Gongen appear only in the early modern period. Before that, Matsuo-ji had its main hall at the current site of Hongū (本宮), enshrining an Eleven-Headed Kannon (十一面観音菩薩) as its principal deity. A shrine to the Thirty Guardian Deities (三十番神) was also located nearby. During the late 16th century, Matsuo-ji underwent significant transformations. A monk named Yūga (宥雅), a disciple of [[:en:Zentsū-ji|Zentsū-ji]], became the temple's abbot and founded Konkō-in (金光院). In 1573, he established a hall for Konpira Gongen (金毘羅堂) at the foot of the temple complex, separate from the original temple structure. In 1579, when [[:en:Chōsokabe Motochika|Chōsokabe Motochika]] sought to control religious institutions in Sanuki, Yūga fled, and the Chōsokabe forces appointed the monk Yūgen (宥厳) as the new abbot. During the Edo period, Konpira Gongen's faith expanded, and Matsuo-ji became a prominent center of pilgrimage. In 1813, the temple's Yakushi Hall (薬師堂) was rebuilt into a grand structure, housing a [[:en:Bhaisajyaguru|Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha]] statue and the Twelve Divine Generals (薬師十二神将). The temple flourished under the patronage of various regional rulers, and Konpira shrines spread throughout Japan. === Meiji Restoration and the Separation of Shinto and Buddhism === During the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) policies led to significant changes at Matsuo-ji. The temple complex was restructured: - The mountain name was changed from Zōzusan (象頭山) to Kotohirayama (琴平山). - The temple village was renamed from Konpira Village (金毘羅村) to Kotohira Village (琴平村). - The shrine was renamed from Konpira Gongen (金毘羅大権現) to [[:en:Kotohira-gū|Kotohira-gū]]. The temple itself was largely dismantled, with only Fumon-in (普門院) remaining under the name Matsuo-ji. Many Buddhist structures were converted into Shinto shrines or destroyed. However, some Buddhist artifacts, such as a Kamakura-period statue of {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}}, were preserved and enshrined in the temple. == Temple Grounds == [[File:Matuojo 20191125 01.jpg|thumb|180px|Main Gate (Sanmon)]] The temple complex includes the following structures: * Sanmon (temple gate) * Main Hall (本堂) * Bell Tower (鐘楼堂) * Kuri (庫裡, temple living quarters) == Cultural Properties == '''Kagawa Prefecture Designated Cultural Properties''' * '''Seated Statue of Kōbō Daishi''' (木造弘法大師座像) – Created in 1319 (Bunpō 3), made of cypress wood (檜材), designated on March 31, 2009. * '''Wooden Five-Ring Pagoda''' (木造五輪塔) – Contains an inscription dated 1604 (Keichō 9), associated with the monk Yūsei (宥盛), designated on March 31, 2009. == location == * '''Address:''' 973 Kawanishi, Kotohira, Nakatado District, Kagawa Prefecture, Japan (香川県仲多度郡琴平町字川西973番地) == Pilgrimage == * '''Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage''' ''' 15: {{ill|Kenshō-ji (Marugame)|ja|顕正寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenshō-ji (Marugame)|qq=|1=Kenshō-ji (Marugame)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665121}} → '''16: Matsuo-ji''' → 17: {{ill|Butsumo-in|ja|仏母院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Butsumo-in|qq=|1=Butsumoin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11377550}} == References == === Notes === {{Notelist}} === Sources === {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[:en:Konpira Gongen|Konpira Gongen]] == External Links == * [http://www.mandala88.com/ Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage Website] {{translated page|ja|松尾寺 (香川県琴平町)|version=98510282|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Buddhist temples in Kagawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Kōyasan Shingon temples]] [[Category:En no Gyōja]] [[Category:Kotohira, Kagawa]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Kotohira shrines]] }} [[zh:松尾寺 (琴平町)]] [[en:Matsuo-ji]] [[sn:E6%9D%BE%E5%B0%BE%E5%AF%BA (%E9%A6%99%E5%B7%9D%E7%9C%8C%E7%90%B4%E5%B9%B3%E7%94%BA)]] [[sn:Matsuo-ji]] [[Category:Bettoji Temples]] [[Category:New Shikoku Mandala Sacred Sites]] [[Category:Buildings in Kotohira Town]] The temple complex was restructured: - The mountain name was changed from Zōzusan (象頭山) to Kotohirayama (琴平山). - The temple village was renamed from Konpira Village (金毘羅村) to Kotohira Village (琴平村). - The shrine was renamed from Konpira Gongen (金毘羅大権現) to [[:en:Kotohira-gū|Kotohira-gū]]. The temple itself was largely dismantled, with only Fumon-in (普門院) remaining under the name Matsuo-ji. Many Buddhist structures were converted into Shinto shrines or destroyed. However, some Buddhist artifacts, such as a Kamakura-period statue of {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}}, were preserved and enshrined in the temple. == Temple Grounds == [[File:Matuojo 20191125 01.jpg|thumb|180px|Main Gate (Sanmon)]] The temple complex includes the following structures: * Sanmon (temple gate) * Main Hall (本堂) * Bell Tower (鐘楼堂) * Kuri (庫裡, temple living quarters) == Cultural Properties == '''Kagawa Prefecture Designated Cultural Properties''' * '''Seated Statue of Kōbō Daishi''' (木造弘法大師座像) – Created in 1319 (Bunpō 3), made of cypress wood (檜材), designated on March 31, 2009. * '''Wooden Five-Ring Pagoda''' (木造五輪塔) – Contains an inscription dated 1604 (Keichō 9), associated with the monk Yūsei (宥盛), designated on March 31, 2009. == location == * '''Address:''' 973 Kawanishi, Kotohira, Nakatado District, Kagawa Prefecture, Japan (香川県仲多度郡琴平町字川西973番地) == [[commons:Category:Matsuo-ji (Kotohira)]][[en:Matsuo-ji]][[ja:松尾寺 (香川県琴平町)]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Matuozi02.jpg|religious_affiliation={{ill|Kōyasan Shingon-shū|qid=Q3077357}}|prefecture={{ill|Kotohira|qid=Q1271483}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.185778|N|133.818389|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|deity={{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qid=Q11644537}}|part_of={{ill|Shin-Shikoku Mandara Pilgrimage|qid=Q11501519}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11529610]] Wikidata name: Matsuo-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:20, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Japanese temple infobox | Name = Matsuo-ji (松尾寺) | image = [[File:Matuozi02.jpg|250px]]
Main Hall | location = [[:en:Kotohira, Kagawa|Kotohira, Kagawa]], [[:en:Nakatado District, Kagawa|Nakatado District, Kagawa]], [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{Coord|34|11|8.8|N|133|49|6.2|E}} | Mount name = Zōzusan (象頭山) | Sect = [[:en:Kōyasan Shingon-shū|Kōyasan Shingon-shū]] | Main deity = {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}} | Founded = [[:en:Taiho|Taihō era]] (701–704 AD) | Founder = [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]] | Official name = Zōzusan Fumon-in Matsuo-ji (象頭山 普門院 松尾寺) | Pilgrimage = {{ill|Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|ja|新四国曼荼羅霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|qq=|1=Shin-Shikoku Mandara Pilgrimage|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11501519}} No.16 }} '''Matsuo-ji''' (松尾寺) is a [[:en:Kōyasan Shingon-shū|Kōyasan Shingon-shū]] temple located in [[:en:Kotohira, Kagawa|Kotohira, Kagawa]], [[:en:Nakatado District, Kagawa|Nakatado District, Kagawa]], [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]]. The temple's mountain name (山号) is Zōzusan (象頭山), and its cloister name (院号) is Fumon-in (普門院). The principal deity is {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}}, and it is the 16th temple in the {{ill|Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|ja|新四国曼荼羅霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|qq=|1=Shin-Shikoku Mandara Pilgrimage|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11501519}}. '''Temple hymn (Goeika, ご詠歌):''' *"Miagureba / kokoro mo kiyoki / Zōzusan / kin no gohei ni / kami zo masumasu"* (*"Looking up, / even the heart is purified, / Zōzusan, / with golden gohei, / the deity is ever-present."*) == History == [[File:Matuoji 20191125 02.jpg|thumb|180px|Sanmon (temple gate)]] According to the temple's legend, Matsuo-ji was founded in the [[:en:Taiho|Taihō era]] (701–704 AD) when [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]], the founder of [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendō]], climbed Mount Zōzusan. It is said that he received a divine revelation from {{ill|Kumbhīra (mythology)|ja|マカラ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumbhīra (mythology)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to マカラ (神話)|1=Makara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1136842}}, a protective deity (護法善神) dwelling in [[:en:Vulture Peak|Vulture Peak]] in India, which later became associated with [[:en:Konpira Gongen|Konpira Gongen]].Short history of Matsuo-ji However, many historical records were lost during the invasion of [[:en:Chōsokabe Motochika|Chōsokabe Motochika]] into [[:en:Sanuki Province|Sanuki Province]]. The earliest records of Konpira Gongen appear only in the early modern period. Before that, Matsuo-ji had its main hall at the current site of Hongū (本宮), enshrining an Eleven-Headed Kannon (十一面観音菩薩) as its principal deity. A shrine to the Thirty Guardian Deities (三十番神) was also located nearby. During the late 16th century, Matsuo-ji underwent significant transformations. A monk named Yūga (宥雅), a disciple of [[:en:Zentsū-ji|Zentsū-ji]], became the temple's abbot and founded Konkō-in (金光院). In 1573, he established a hall for Konpira Gongen (金毘羅堂) at the foot of the temple complex, separate from the original temple structure. In 1579, when [[:en:Chōsokabe Motochika|Chōsokabe Motochika]] sought to control religious institutions in Sanuki, Yūga fled, and the Chōsokabe forces appointed the monk Yūgen (宥厳) as the new abbot. During the Edo period, Konpira Gongen's faith expanded, and Matsuo-ji became a prominent center of pilgrimage. In 1813, the temple's Yakushi Hall (薬師堂) was rebuilt into a grand structure, housing a [[:en:Bhaisajyaguru|Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha]] statue and the Twelve Divine Generals (薬師十二神将). The temple flourished under the patronage of various regional rulers, and Konpira shrines spread throughout Japan. === Meiji Restoration and the Separation of Shinto and Buddhism === During the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) policies led to significant changes at Matsuo-ji. The temple complex was restructured: - The mountain name was changed from Zōzusan (象頭山) to Kotohirayama (琴平山). - The temple village was renamed from Konpira Village (金毘羅村) to Kotohira Village (琴平村). - The shrine was renamed from Konpira Gongen (金毘羅大権現) to [[:en:Kotohira-gū|Kotohira-gū]]. The temple itself was largely dismantled, with only Fumon-in (普門院) remaining under the name Matsuo-ji. Many Buddhist structures were converted into Shinto shrines or destroyed. However, some Buddhist artifacts, such as a Kamakura-period statue of {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}}, were preserved and enshrined in the temple. == Temple Grounds == [[File:Matuojo 20191125 01.jpg|thumb|180px|Main Gate (Sanmon)]] The temple complex includes the following structures: * Sanmon (temple gate) * Main Hall (本堂) * Bell Tower (鐘楼堂) * Kuri (庫裡, temple living quarters) == Cultural Properties == '''Kagawa Prefecture Designated Cultural Properties''' * '''Seated Statue of Kōbō Daishi''' (木造弘法大師座像) – Created in 1319 (Bunpō 3), made of cypress wood (檜材), designated on March 31, 2009. * '''Wooden Five-Ring Pagoda''' (木造五輪塔) – Contains an inscription dated 1604 (Keichō 9), associated with the monk Yūsei (宥盛), designated on March 31, 2009. == location == * '''Address:''' 973 Kawanishi, Kotohira, Nakatado District, Kagawa Prefecture, Japan (香川県仲多度郡琴平町字川西973番地) == [[sn:commons:Category:Matsuo-ji (Kotohira)]][[en:Matsuo-ji]][[ja:松尾寺 (香川県琴平町)]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Matuozi02.jpg|religious_affiliation={{ill|Kōyasan Shingon-shū|WD=Q3077357}}|prefecture={{ill|Kotohira|WD=Q1271483}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.185778|N|133.818389|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|deity={{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|WD=Q11644537}}|part_of={{ill|Shin-Shikoku Mandara Pilgrimage|WD=Q11501519}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11529610]] Wikidata name: Matsuo-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:20, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Japanese temple infobox | Name = Matsuo-ji (松尾寺) | image = [[File:Matuozi02.jpg|250px]]
Main Hall | location = [[:en:Kotohira, Kagawa|Kotohira, Kagawa]], [[:en:Nakatado District, Kagawa|Nakatado District, Kagawa]], [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{Coord|34|11|8.8|N|133|49|6.2|E}} | Mount name = Zōzusan (象頭山) | Sect = [[:en:Kōyasan Shingon-shū|Kōyasan Shingon-shū]] | Main deity = {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}} | Founded = [[:en:Taiho|Taihō era]] (701–704 AD) | Founder = [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]] | Official name = Zōzusan Fumon-in Matsuo-ji (象頭山 普門院 松尾寺) | Pilgrimage = {{ill|Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|ja|新四国曼荼羅霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|qq=|1=Shin-Shikoku Mandara Pilgrimage|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11501519}} No.16 }} '''Matsuo-ji''' (松尾寺) is a [[:en:Kōyasan Shingon-shū|Kōyasan Shingon-shū]] temple located in [[:en:Kotohira, Kagawa|Kotohira, Kagawa]], [[:en:Nakatado District, Kagawa|Nakatado District, Kagawa]], [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]]. The temple's mountain name (山号) is Zōzusan (象頭山), and its cloister name (院号) is Fumon-in (普門院). The principal deity is {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}}, and it is the 16th temple in the {{ill|Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|ja|新四国曼荼羅霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|qq=|1=Shin-Shikoku Mandara Pilgrimage|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11501519}}. '''Temple hymn (Goeika, ご詠歌):''' *"Miagureba / kokoro mo kiyoki / Zōzusan / kin no gohei ni / kami zo masumasu"* (*"Looking up, / even the heart is purified, / Zōzusan, / with golden gohei, / the deity is ever-present."*) == History == [[File:Matuoji 20191125 02.jpg|thumb|180px|Sanmon (temple gate)]] According to the temple's legend, Matsuo-ji was founded in the [[:en:Taiho|Taihō era]] (701–704 AD) when [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]], the founder of [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendō]], climbed Mount Zōzusan. It is said that he received a divine revelation from {{ill|Kumbhīra (mythology)|ja|マカラ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumbhīra (mythology)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to マカラ (神話)|1=Makara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1136842}}, a protective deity (護法善神) dwelling in [[:en:Vulture Peak|Vulture Peak]] in India, which later became associated with [[:en:Konpira Gongen|Konpira Gongen]].Short history of Matsuo-ji However, many historical records were lost during the invasion of [[:en:Chōsokabe Motochika|Chōsokabe Motochika]] into [[:en:Sanuki Province|Sanuki Province]]. The earliest records of Konpira Gongen appear only in the early modern period. Before that, Matsuo-ji had its main hall at the current site of Hongū (本宮), enshrining an Eleven-Headed Kannon (十一面観音菩薩) as its principal deity. A shrine to the Thirty Guardian Deities (三十番神) was also located nearby. During the late 16th century, Matsuo-ji underwent significant transformations. A monk named Yūga (宥雅), a disciple of [[:en:Zentsū-ji|Zentsū-ji]], became the temple's abbot and founded Konkō-in (金光院). In 1573, he established a hall for Konpira Gongen (金毘羅堂) at the foot of the temple complex, separate from the original temple structure. In 1579, when [[:en:Chōsokabe Motochika|Chōsokabe Motochika]] sought to control religious institutions in Sanuki, Yūga fled, and the Chōsokabe forces appointed the monk Yūgen (宥厳) as the new abbot. During the Edo period, Konpira Gongen's faith expanded, and Matsuo-ji became a prominent center of pilgrimage. In 1813, the temple's Yakushi Hall (薬師堂) was rebuilt into a grand structure, housing a [[:en:Bhaisajyaguru|Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha]] statue and the Twelve Divine Generals (薬師十二神将). The temple flourished under the patronage of various regional rulers, and Konpira shrines spread throughout Japan. === Meiji Restoration and the Separation of Shinto and Buddhism === During the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) policies led to significant changes at Matsuo-ji. The temple complex was restructured: - The mountain name was changed from Zōzusan (象頭山) to Kotohirayama (琴平山). - The temple village was renamed from Konpira Village (金毘羅村) to Kotohira Village (琴平村). - The shrine was renamed from Konpira Gongen (金毘羅大権現) to [[:en:Kotohira-gū|Kotohira-gū]]. The temple itself was largely dismantled, with only Fumon-in (普門院) remaining under the name Matsuo-ji. Many Buddhist structures were converted into Shinto shrines or destroyed. However, some Buddhist artifacts, such as a Kamakura-period statue of {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}}, were preserved and enshrined in the temple. == Temple Grounds == [[File:Matuojo 20191125 01.jpg|thumb|180px|Main Gate (Sanmon)]] The temple complex includes the following structures: * Sanmon (temple gate) * Main Hall (本堂) * Bell Tower (鐘楼堂) * Kuri (庫裡, temple living quarters) == Cultural Properties == '''Kagawa Prefecture Designated Cultural Properties''' * '''Seated Statue of Kōbō Daishi''' (木造弘法大師座像) – Created in 1319 (Bunpō 3), made of cypress wood (檜材), designated on March 31, 2009. * '''Wooden Five-Ring Pagoda''' (木造五輪塔) – Contains an inscription dated 1604 (Keichō 9), associated with the monk Yūsei (宥盛), designated on March 31, 2009. == location == * '''Address:''' 973 Kawanishi, Kotohira, Nakatado District, Kagawa Prefecture, Japan (香川県仲多度郡琴平町字川西973番地) == Pilgrimage == * '''Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage''' ''' 15: {{ill|Kenshō-ji (Marugame)|ja|顕正寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenshō-ji (Marugame)|qq=|1=Kenshō-ji (Marugame)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665121}} → '''16: Matsuo-ji''' → 17: {{ill|Butsumo-in|ja|仏母院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Butsumo-in|qq=|1=Butsumoin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11377550}} == References == === Notes === {{Notelist}} === The temple complex was restructured: - The mountain name was changed from Zōzusan (象頭山) to Kotohirayama (琴平山). - The temple village was renamed from Konpira Village (金毘羅村) to Kotohira Village (琴平村). - The shrine was renamed from Konpira Gongen (金毘羅大権現) to [[:en:Kotohira-gū|Kotohira-gū]]. The temple itself was largely dismantled, with only Fumon-in (普門院) remaining under the name Matsuo-ji. Many Buddhist structures were converted into Shinto shrines or destroyed. However, some Buddhist artifacts, such as a Kamakura-period statue of {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}}, were preserved and enshrined in the temple. == Temple Grounds == [[File:Matuojo 20191125 01.jpg|thumb|180px|Main Gate (Sanmon)]] The temple complex includes the following structures: * Sanmon (temple gate) * Main Hall (本堂) * Bell Tower (鐘楼堂) * Kuri (庫裡, temple living quarters) == Cultural Properties == '''Kagawa Prefecture Designated Cultural Properties''' * '''Seated Statue of Kōbō Daishi''' (木造弘法大師座像) – Created in 1319 (Bunpō 3), made of cypress wood (檜材), designated on March 31, 2009. * '''Wooden Five-Ring Pagoda''' (木造五輪塔) – Contains an inscription dated 1604 (Keichō 9), associated with the monk Yūsei (宥盛), designated on March 31, 2009. == location == * '''Address:''' 973 Kawanishi, Kotohira, Nakatado District, Kagawa Prefecture, Japan (香川県仲多度郡琴平町字川西973番地) == Pilgrimage == * '''Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage''' ''' 15: {{ill|Kenshō-ji (Marugame)|ja|顕正寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenshō-ji (Marugame)|1=Kenshō-ji (Marugame)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665121}} → '''16: Matsuo-ji''' → 17: {{ill|Butsumo-in|ja|仏母院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Butsumo-in|1=Butsumoin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11377550}} == References == === Notes === {{Notelist}} === Sources === {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[:en:Konpira Gongen|Konpira Gongen]] == External Links == * [http://www.mandala88.com/ Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage Website] {{translated page|ja|松尾寺 (香川県琴平町)|version=98510282|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Buddhist temples in [[sn:commons:Category:Matsuo-ji (Kotohira)]][[en:Matsuo-ji]][[ja:松尾寺 (香川県琴平町)]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Matuozi02.jpg|religious_affiliation={{ill|Kōyasan Shingon-shū|WD=Q3077357}}|prefecture={{ill|Kotohira|WD=Q1271483}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.185778|N|133.818389|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|deity={{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|WD=Q11644537}}|part_of={{ill|Shin-Shikoku Mandara Pilgrimage|WD=Q11501519}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11529610]] Wikidata name: Matsuo-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:20, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Japanese temple infobox | Name = Matsuo-ji (松尾寺) | image = [[File:Matuozi02.jpg|250px]]
Main Hall | location = [[:en:Kotohira, Kagawa|Kotohira, Kagawa]], [[:en:Nakatado District, Kagawa|Nakatado District, Kagawa]], [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{Coord|34|11|8.8|N|133|49|6.2|E}} | Mount name = Zōzusan (象頭山) | Sect = [[:en:Kōyasan Shingon-shū|Kōyasan Shingon-shū]] | Main deity = {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}} | Founded = [[:en:Taiho|Taihō era]] (701–704 AD) | Founder = [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]] | Official name = Zōzusan Fumon-in Matsuo-ji (象頭山 普門院 松尾寺) | Pilgrimage = {{ill|Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|ja|新四国曼荼羅霊場|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|1=Shin-Shikoku Mandara Pilgrimage|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11501519}} No.16 }} '''Matsuo-ji''' (松尾寺) is a [[:en:Kōyasan Shingon-shū|Kōyasan Shingon-shū]] temple located in [[:en:Kotohira, Kagawa|Kotohira, Kagawa]], [[:en:Nakatado District, Kagawa|Nakatado District, Kagawa]], [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]]. The temple's mountain name (山号) is Zōzusan (象頭山), and its cloister name (院号) is Fumon-in (普門院). The principal deity is {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}}, and it is the 16th temple in the {{ill|Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|ja|新四国曼荼羅霊場|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|1=Shin-Shikoku Mandara Pilgrimage|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11501519}}. '''Temple hymn (Goeika, ご詠歌):''' *"Miagureba / kokoro mo kiyoki / Zōzusan / kin no gohei ni / kami zo masumasu"* (*"Looking up, / even the heart is purified, / Zōzusan, / with golden gohei, / the deity is ever-present."*) == History == [[File:Matuoji 20191125 02.jpg|thumb|180px|Sanmon (temple gate)]] According to the temple's legend, Matsuo-ji was founded in the [[:en:Taiho|Taihō era]] (701–704 AD) when [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]], the founder of [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendō]], climbed Mount Zōzusan. It is said that he received a divine revelation from {{ill|Kumbhīra (mythology)|ja|マカラ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumbhīra (mythology)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to マカラ (神話)|1=Makara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1136842}}, a protective deity (護法善神) dwelling in [[:en:Vulture Peak|Vulture Peak]] in India, which later became associated with [[:en:Konpira Gongen|Konpira Gongen]].Short history of Matsuo-ji However, many historical records were lost during the invasion of [[:en:Chōsokabe Motochika|Chōsokabe Motochika]] into [[:en:Sanuki Province|Sanuki Province]]. The earliest records of Konpira Gongen appear only in the early modern period. Before that, Matsuo-ji had its main hall at the current site of Hongū (本宮), enshrining an Eleven-Headed Kannon (十一面観音菩薩) as its principal deity. A shrine to the Thirty Guardian Deities (三十番神) was also located nearby. During the late 16th century, Matsuo-ji underwent significant transformations. A monk named Yūga (宥雅), a disciple of [[:en:Zentsū-ji|Zentsū-ji]], became the temple's abbot and founded Konkō-in (金光院). In 1573, he established a hall for Konpira Gongen (金毘羅堂) at the foot of the temple complex, separate from the original temple structure. In 1579, when [[:en:Chōsokabe Motochika|Chōsokabe Motochika]] sought to control religious institutions in Sanuki, Yūga fled, and the Chōsokabe forces appointed the monk Yūgen (宥厳) as the new abbot. During the Edo period, Konpira Gongen's faith expanded, and Matsuo-ji became a prominent center of pilgrimage. In 1813, the temple's Yakushi Hall (薬師堂) was rebuilt into a grand structure, housing a [[:en:Bhaisajyaguru|Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha]] statue and the Twelve Divine Generals (薬師十二神将). The temple flourished under the patronage of various regional rulers, and Konpira shrines spread throughout Japan. === Meiji Restoration and the Separation of Shinto and Buddhism === During the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) policies led to significant changes at Matsuo-ji. The temple complex was restructured: - The mountain name was changed from Zōzusan (象頭山) to Kotohirayama (琴平山). - The temple village was renamed from Konpira Village (金毘羅村) to Kotohira Village (琴平村). - The shrine was renamed from Konpira Gongen (金毘羅大権現) to [[:en:Kotohira-gū|Kotohira-gū]]. The temple itself was largely dismantled, with only Fumon-in (普門院) remaining under the name Matsuo-ji. Many Buddhist structures were converted into Shinto shrines or destroyed. However, some Buddhist artifacts, such as a Kamakura-period statue of {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}}, were preserved and enshrined in the temple. == Temple Grounds == [[File:Matuojo 20191125 01.jpg|thumb|180px|Main Gate (Sanmon)]] The temple complex includes the following structures: * Sanmon (temple gate) * Main Hall (本堂) * Bell Tower (鐘楼堂) * Kuri (庫裡, temple living quarters) == Cultural Properties == '''Kagawa Prefecture Designated Cultural Properties''' * '''Seated Statue of Kōbō Daishi''' (木造弘法大師座像) – Created in 1319 (Bunpō 3), made of cypress wood (檜材), designated on March 31, 2009. * '''Wooden Five-Ring Pagoda''' (木造五輪塔) – Contains an inscription dated 1604 (Keichō 9), associated with the monk Yūsei (宥盛), designated on March 31, 2009. == location == * '''Address:''' 973 Kawanishi, Kotohira, Nakatado District, Kagawa Prefecture, Japan (香川県仲多度郡琴平町字川西973番地) == Pilgrimage == * '''Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage''' ''' 15: {{ill|Kenshō-ji (Marugame)|ja|顕正寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenshō-ji (Marugame)|1=Kenshō-ji (Marugame)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665121}} → '''16: Matsuo-ji''' → 17: {{ill|Butsumo-in|ja|仏母院|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Butsumo-in|1=Butsumoin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11377550}} == References == === Notes === [[sn:commons:Category:Matsuo-ji (Kotohira)]][[en:Matsuo-ji]][[ja:松尾寺 (香川県琴平町)]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Matuozi02.jpg|religious_affiliation={{ill|Kōyasan Shingon-shū|WD=Q3077357}}|prefecture={{ill|Kotohira|WD=Q1271483}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.185778|N|133.818389|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|deity={{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|WD=Q11644537}}|part_of={{ill|Shin-Shikoku Mandara Pilgrimage|WD=Q11501519}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11529610]] Wikidata name: Matsuo-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:20, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Japanese temple infobox | Name = Matsuo-ji (松尾寺) | image = [[File:Matuozi02.jpg|250px]]
Main Hall | location = [[:en:Kotohira, Kagawa|Kotohira, Kagawa]], [[:en:Nakatado District, Kagawa|Nakatado District, Kagawa]], [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{Coord|34|11|8.8|N|133|49|6.2|E}} | Mount name = Zōzusan (象頭山) | Sect = [[:en:Kōyasan Shingon-shū|Kōyasan Shingon-shū]] | Main deity = {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}} | Founded = [[:en:Taiho|Taihō era]] (701–704 AD) | Founder = [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]] | Official name = Zōzusan Fumon-in Matsuo-ji (象頭山 普門院 松尾寺) | Pilgrimage = {{ill|Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|ja|新四国曼荼羅霊場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|1=Shin-Shikoku Mandara Pilgrimage|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11501519}} No.16 }} '''Matsuo-ji''' (松尾寺) is a [[:en:Kōyasan Shingon-shū|Kōyasan Shingon-shū]] temple located in [[:en:Kotohira, Kagawa|Kotohira, Kagawa]], [[:en:Nakatado District, Kagawa|Nakatado District, Kagawa]], [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]]. The temple's mountain name (山号) is Zōzusan (象頭山), and its cloister name (院号) is Fumon-in (普門院). The principal deity is {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}}, and it is the 16th temple in the {{ill|Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|ja|新四国曼荼羅霊場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|1=Shin-Shikoku Mandara Pilgrimage|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11501519}}. '''Temple hymn (Goeika, ご詠歌):''' *"Miagureba / kokoro mo kiyoki / Zōzusan / kin no gohei ni / kami zo masumasu"* (*"Looking up, / even the heart is purified, / Zōzusan, / with golden gohei, / the deity is ever-present."*) == History == [[File:Matuoji 20191125 02.jpg|thumb|180px|Sanmon (temple gate)]] According to the temple's legend, Matsuo-ji was founded in the [[:en:Taiho|Taihō era]] (701–704 AD) when [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]], the founder of [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendō]], climbed Mount Zōzusan. It is said that he received a divine revelation from {{ill|Kumbhīra (mythology)|ja|マカラ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumbhīra (mythology)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to マカラ (神話)|1=Makara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1136842}}, a protective deity (護法善神) dwelling in [[:en:Vulture Peak|Vulture Peak]] in India, which later became associated with [[:en:Konpira Gongen|Konpira Gongen]].Short history of Matsuo-ji However, many historical records were lost during the invasion of [[:en:Chōsokabe Motochika|Chōsokabe Motochika]] into [[:en:Sanuki Province|Sanuki Province]]. The earliest records of Konpira Gongen appear only in the early modern period. Before that, Matsuo-ji had its main hall at the current site of Hongū (本宮), enshrining an Eleven-Headed Kannon (十一面観音菩薩) as its principal deity. A shrine to the Thirty Guardian Deities (三十番神) was also located nearby. During the late 16th century, Matsuo-ji underwent significant transformations. A monk named Yūga (宥雅), a disciple of [[:en:Zentsū-ji|Zentsū-ji]], became the temple's abbot and founded Konkō-in (金光院). In 1573, he established a hall for Konpira Gongen (金毘羅堂) at the foot of the temple complex, separate from the original temple structure. In 1579, when [[:en:Chōsokabe Motochika|Chōsokabe Motochika]] sought to control religious institutions in Sanuki, Yūga fled, and the Chōsokabe forces appointed the monk Yūgen (宥厳) as the new abbot. During the Edo period, Konpira Gongen's faith expanded, and Matsuo-ji became a prominent center of pilgrimage. In 1813, the temple's Yakushi Hall (薬師堂) was rebuilt into a grand structure, housing a [[:en:Bhaisajyaguru|Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha]] statue and the Twelve Divine Generals (薬師十二神将). The temple flourished under the patronage of various regional rulers, and Konpira shrines spread throughout Japan. === Meiji Restoration and the Separation of Shinto and Buddhism === During the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) policies led to significant changes at Matsuo-ji. The temple complex was restructured: - The mountain name was changed from Zōzusan (象頭山) to Kotohirayama (琴平山). - The temple village was renamed from Konpira Village (金毘羅村) to Kotohira Village (琴平村). - The shrine was renamed from Konpira Gongen (金毘羅大権現) to [[:en:Kotohira-gū|Kotohira-gū]]. The temple itself was largely dismantled, with only Fumon-in (普門院) remaining under the name Matsuo-ji. Many Buddhist structures were converted into Shinto shrines or destroyed. However, some Buddhist artifacts, such as a Kamakura-period statue of {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}}, were preserved and enshrined in the temple. == Temple Grounds == [[File:Matuojo 20191125 01.jpg|thumb|180px|Main Gate (Sanmon)]] The temple complex includes the following structures: * Sanmon (temple gate) * Main Hall (本堂) * Bell Tower (鐘楼堂) * Kuri (庫裡, temple living quarters) == Cultural Properties == '''Kagawa Prefecture Designated Cultural Properties''' * '''Seated Statue of Kōbō Daishi''' (木造弘法大師座像) – Created in 1319 (Bunpō 3), made of cypress wood (檜材), designated on March 31, 2009. * '''Wooden Five-Ring Pagoda''' (木造五輪塔) – Contains an inscription dated 1604 (Keichō 9), associated with the monk Yūsei (宥盛), designated on March 31, 2009. == location == * '''Address:''' 973 Kawanishi, Kotohira, Nakatado District, Kagawa Prefecture, Japan (香川県仲多度郡琴平町字川西973番地) == Pilgrimage == * '''Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage''' ''' 15: {{ill|Kenshō-ji (Marugame)|ja|顕正寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenshō-ji (Marugame)|1=Kenshō-ji (Marugame)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665121}} → '''16: Matsuo-ji''' → 17: {{ill|Butsumo-in|ja|仏母院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Butsumo-in|1=Butsumoin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11377550}} == References == === Notes === {{Notelist}} === Sources === {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[:en:Konpira Gongen|Konpira Gongen]] == External Links == * [http://www.mandala88.com/ Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage Website] {{translated page|ja|松尾寺 (香川県琴平町)|version=98510282|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Buddhist temples in Kagawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Kōyasan Shingon temples]] [[Category:En no Gyōja]] [[Category:Kotohira, Kagawa]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Kotohira shrines]] }} [[zh:松尾寺 (琴平町)]] [[en:Matsuo-ji]] [[Category:Bettoji Temples]] [[Category:New Shikoku Mandala Sacred Sites]] [[Category:Buildings in Kotohira Town]] [[Category:History of Kotohira Town]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] {{Commons category|Matsuo-ji (Kotohira)}} [[ja:松尾寺 (香川県琴平町)]] {{Commons category|Matsuo-ji (Kotohira)}} [[ja:松尾寺 (香川県琴平町)]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] {{wikidata link|Q11529610}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

However, some Buddhist artifacts, such as a Kamakura-period statue of {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}}, were preserved and enshrined in the temple. == Temple Grounds == [[File:Matuojo 20191125 01.jpg|thumb|180px|Main Gate (Sanmon)]] The temple complex includes the following structures: * Sanmon (temple gate) * Main Hall (本堂) * Bell Tower (鐘楼堂) * Kuri (庫裡, temple living quarters) == Cultural Properties == '''Kagawa Prefecture Designated Cultural Properties''' * '''Seated Statue of Kōbō Daishi''' (木造弘法大師座像) – Created in 1319 (Bunpō 3), made of cypress wood (檜材), designated on March 31, 2009. * '''Wooden Five-Ring Pagoda''' (木造五輪塔) – Contains an inscription dated 1604 (Keichō 9), associated with the monk Yūsei (宥盛), designated on March 31, 2009. == location == * '''Address:''' 973 Kawanishi, Kotohira, Nakatado District, Kagawa Prefecture, Japan (香川県仲多度郡琴平町字川西973番地) == Pilgrimage == * '''Shikoku [[sn:commons:Category:Matsuo-ji (Kotohira)]][[en:Matsuo-ji]][[ja:松尾寺 (香川県琴平町)]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Matuozi02.jpg|religious_affiliation={{ill|Kōyasan Shingon-shū|WD=Q3077357}}|prefecture={{ill|Kotohira|WD=Q1271483}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.185778|N|133.818389|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|deity={{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|WD=Q11644537}}|part_of={{ill|Shin-Shikoku Mandara Pilgrimage|WD=Q11501519}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11529610]] Wikidata name: Matsuo-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:20, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Japanese temple infobox | Name = Matsuo-ji (松尾寺) | image = [[File:Matuozi02.jpg|250px]]
Main Hall | location = [[:en:Kotohira, Kagawa|Kotohira, Kagawa]], [[:en:Nakatado District, Kagawa|Nakatado District, Kagawa]], [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{Coord|34|11|8.8|N|133|49|6.2|E}} | Mount name = Zōzusan (象頭山) | Sect = [[:en:Kōyasan Shingon-shū|Kōyasan Shingon-shū]] | Main deity = {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}} | Founded = [[:en:Taiho|Taihō era]] (701–704 AD) | Founder = [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]] | Official name = Zōzusan Fumon-in Matsuo-ji (象頭山 普門院 松尾寺) | Pilgrimage = {{ill|Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|ja|新四国曼荼羅霊場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|1=Shin-Shikoku Mandara Pilgrimage|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11501519}} No.16 }} '''Matsuo-ji''' (松尾寺) is a [[:en:Kōyasan Shingon-shū|Kōyasan Shingon-shū]] temple located in [[:en:Kotohira, Kagawa|Kotohira, Kagawa]], [[:en:Nakatado District, Kagawa|Nakatado District, Kagawa]], [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]]. The temple's mountain name (山号) is Zōzusan (象頭山), and its cloister name (院号) is Fumon-in (普門院). The principal deity is {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}}, and it is the 16th temple in the {{ill|Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|ja|新四国曼荼羅霊場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|1=Shin-Shikoku Mandara Pilgrimage|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11501519}}. '''Temple hymn (Goeika, ご詠歌):''' *"Miagureba / kokoro mo kiyoki / Zōzusan / kin no gohei ni / kami zo masumasu"* (*"Looking up, / even the heart is purified, / Zōzusan, / with golden gohei, / the deity is ever-present."*) == History == [[File:Matuoji 20191125 02.jpg|thumb|180px|Sanmon (temple gate)]] According to the temple's legend, Matsuo-ji was founded in the [[:en:Taiho|Taihō era]] (701–704 AD) when [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]], the founder of [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendō]], climbed Mount Zōzusan. It is said that he received a divine revelation from {{ill|Kumbhīra (mythology)|ja|マカラ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumbhīra (mythology)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to マカラ (神話)|1=Makara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1136842}}, a protective deity (護法善神) dwelling in [[:en:Vulture Peak|Vulture Peak]] in India, which later became associated with [[:en:Konpira Gongen|Konpira Gongen]].Short history of Matsuo-ji However, many historical records were lost during the invasion of [[:en:Chōsokabe Motochika|Chōsokabe Motochika]] into [[:en:Sanuki Province|Sanuki Province]]. The earliest records of Konpira Gongen appear only in the early modern period. Before that, Matsuo-ji had its main hall at the current site of Hongū (本宮), enshrining an Eleven-Headed Kannon (十一面観音菩薩) as its principal deity. A shrine to the Thirty Guardian Deities (三十番神) was also located nearby. During the late 16th century, Matsuo-ji underwent significant transformations. A monk named Yūga (宥雅), a disciple of [[:en:Zentsū-ji|Zentsū-ji]], became the temple's abbot and founded Konkō-in (金光院). In 1573, he established a hall for Konpira Gongen (金毘羅堂) at the foot of the temple complex, separate from the original temple structure. In 1579, when [[:en:Chōsokabe Motochika|Chōsokabe Motochika]] sought to control religious institutions in Sanuki, Yūga fled, and the Chōsokabe forces appointed the monk Yūgen (宥厳) as the new abbot. During the Edo period, Konpira Gongen's faith expanded, and Matsuo-ji became a prominent center of pilgrimage. In 1813, the temple's Yakushi Hall (薬師堂) was rebuilt into a grand structure, housing a [[:en:Bhaisajyaguru|Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha]] statue and the Twelve Divine Generals (薬師十二神将). The temple flourished under the patronage of various regional rulers, and Konpira shrines spread throughout Japan. === Meiji Restoration and the Separation of Shinto and Buddhism === During the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) policies led to significant changes at Matsuo-ji. The temple complex was restructured: - The mountain name was changed from Zōzusan (象頭山) to Kotohirayama (琴平山). - The temple village was renamed from Konpira Village (金毘羅村) to Kotohira Village (琴平村). - The shrine was renamed from Konpira Gongen (金毘羅大権現) to [[:en:Kotohira-gū|Kotohira-gū]]. The temple itself was largely dismantled, with only Fumon-in (普門院) remaining under the name Matsuo-ji. Many Buddhist structures were converted into Shinto shrines or destroyed. However, some Buddhist artifacts, such as a Kamakura-period statue of {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}}, were preserved and enshrined in the temple. == Temple Grounds == [[File:Matuojo 20191125 01.jpg|thumb|180px|Main Gate (Sanmon)]] The temple complex includes the following structures: * Sanmon (temple gate) * Main Hall (本堂) * Bell Tower (鐘楼堂) * Kuri (庫裡, temple living quarters) == Cultural Properties == '''Kagawa Prefecture Designated Cultural Properties''' * '''Seated Statue of Kōbō Daishi''' (木造弘法大師座像) – Created in 1319 (Bunpō 3), made of cypress wood (檜材), designated on March 31, 2009. * '''Wooden Five-Ring Pagoda''' (木造五輪塔) – Contains an inscription dated 1604 (Keichō 9), associated with the monk Yūsei (宥盛), designated on March 31, 2009. == location == * '''Address:''' 973 Kawanishi, Kotohira, Nakatado District, Kagawa Prefecture, Japan (香川県仲多度郡琴平町字川西973番地) == Pilgrimage == * '''Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage''' ''' 15: {{ill|Kenshō-ji (Marugame)|ja|顕正寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenshō-ji (Marugame)|1=Kenshō-ji (Marugame)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665121}} → '''16: Matsuo-ji''' → 17: {{ill|Butsumo-in|ja|仏母院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Butsumo-in|1=Butsumoin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11377550}} == References == === Notes === {{Notelist}} === The temple's mountain name (山号) is Zōzusan (象頭山), and its cloister name (院号) is Fumon-in (普門院). The principal deity is {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}}, and it is the 16th temple in the {{ill|Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|ja|新四国曼荼羅霊場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|1=Shin-Shikoku Mandara Pilgrimage|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11501519}}. '''Temple hymn (Goeika, ご詠歌):''' *"Miagureba / kokoro mo kiyoki / Zōzusan / kin no gohei ni / kami zo masumasu"* (*"Looking up, / even the heart is purified, / Zōzusan, / with golden gohei, / the deity is ever-present."*) == History == [[File:Matuoji 20191125 02.jpg|thumb|180px|Sanmon (temple gate)]] According to the temple's legend, Matsuo-ji was founded in the [[:en:Taiho|Taihō era]] (701–704 AD) when [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]], the founder of [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendō]], climbed Mount Zōzusan. It is said that he received a divine revelation from {{ill|Kumbhīra (mythology)|ja|マカラ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumbhīra (mythology)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to マカラ (神話)|1=Makara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1136842}}, a protective deity (護法善神) dwelling in [[:en:Vulture Peak|Vulture Peak]] in India, which later became associated with [[:en:Konpira Gongen|Konpira Gongen]].Short history of Matsuo-ji However, many historical records were lost during the invasion of [[:en:Chōsokabe Motochika|Chōsokabe Motochika]] into [[:en:Sanuki Province|Sanuki Province]]. The earliest records of Konpira Gongen appear only in the early modern period. Before that, Matsuo-ji had its main hall at the current site of Hongū (本宮), enshrining an Eleven-Headed Kannon (十一面観音菩薩) as its principal deity. A shrine to the Thirty Guardian Deities (三十番神) was also located nearby. During the late 16th century, Matsuo-ji underwent significant transformations. A monk named Yūga (宥雅), a disciple of [[:en:Zentsū-ji|Zentsū-ji]], became the temple's abbot and founded Konkō-in (金光院). In 1573, he established a hall for Konpira Gongen (金毘羅堂) at the foot of the temple complex, separate from the original temple structure. In 1579, when [[:en:Chōsokabe Motochika|Chōsokabe Motochika]] sought to control religious institutions in Sanuki, Yūga fled, and the Chōsokabe forces appointed the monk Yūgen (宥厳) as the new abbot. During the Edo period, Konpira Gongen's faith expanded, and Matsuo-ji became a prominent center of pilgrimage. In 1813, the temple's Yakushi Hall (薬師堂) was rebuilt into a grand structure, housing a [[:en:Bhaisajyaguru|Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha]] statue and the Twelve Divine Generals (薬師十二神将). The temple flourished under the patronage of various regional rulers, and Konpira shrines spread throughout Japan. === Meiji Restoration and the Separation of Shinto and Buddhism === During the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) policies led to significant changes at Matsuo-ji. The temple complex was restructured: - The mountain name was changed from Zōzusan (象頭山) to Kotohirayama (琴平山). - The temple village was renamed from Konpira Village (金毘羅村) to Kotohira Village (琴平村). - The shrine was renamed from Konpira Gongen (金毘羅大権現) to [[:en:Kotohira-gū|Kotohira-gū]]. The temple itself was largely dismantled, with only Fumon-in (普門院) remaining under the name Matsuo-ji. Many Buddhist structures were converted into Shinto shrines or destroyed. However, some Buddhist artifacts, such as a Kamakura-period statue of {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}}, were preserved and enshrined in the temple. == Temple Grounds == [[File:Matuojo 20191125 01.jpg|thumb|180px|Main Gate (Sanmon)]] The temple complex includes the following structures: * Sanmon (temple gate) * Main Hall (本堂) * Bell Tower (鐘楼堂) * Kuri (庫裡, temple living quarters) == Cultural Properties == '''Kagawa Prefecture Designated Cultural Properties''' * '''Seated Statue of Kōbō Daishi''' (木造弘法大師座像) – Created in 1319 (Bunpō 3), made of cypress wood (檜材), designated on March 31, 2009. * '''Wooden Five-Ring Pagoda''' (木造五輪塔) – Contains an inscription dated 1604 (Keichō 9), associated with the monk Yūsei (宥盛), designated on March 31, 2009. == location == * '''Address:''' 973 Kawanishi, Kotohira, Nakatado District, Kagawa Prefecture, Japan (香川県仲多度郡琴平町字川西973番地) == Pilgrimage == * '''Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage''' ''' 15: {{ill|Kenshō-ji (Marugame)|ja|顕正寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenshō-ji (Marugame)|1=Kenshō-ji (Marugame)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665121}} → '''16: Matsuo-ji''' → 17: {{ill|Butsumo-in|ja|仏母院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Butsumo-in|1=Butsumoin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11377550}} == References == === Notes === {{Notelist}} === Sources === {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[:en:Konpira Gongen|Konpira Gongen]] == External Links == * [http://www.mandala88.com/ Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage Website] {{translated page|ja|松尾寺 (香川県琴平町)|version=98510282|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Buddhist temples in Kagawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Kōyasan Shingon temples]] [[Category:En no Gyōja]] [[Category:Kotohira, Kagawa]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Kotohira shrines]] }} [[zh:松尾寺 (琴平町)]] [[en:Matsuo-ji]] [[Category:Bettoji Temples]] [[Category:New Shikoku Mandala Sacred Sites]] [[Category:Buildings in Kotohira Town]] [[Category:History of Kotohira Town]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] {{Commons category|Matsuo-ji (Kotohira)}} [[ja:松尾寺 (香川県琴平町)]] {{Commons category|Matsuo-ji (Kotohira)}} [[ja:松尾寺 (香川県琴平町)]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] {{wikidata link|Q11529610}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] and enshrined in the temple. == Temple Grounds == [[File:Matuojo 20191125 01.jpg|thumb|180px|Main Gate (Sanmon)]] The temple complex includes the following structures: * Sanmon (temple gate) * Main Created in 1319 (Bunpō 3), made of cypress wood (檜材), designated on March 31, 2009. * '''Wooden Five-Ring Pagoda''' (木造五輪塔) – Contains an inscription dated 1604 (Keichō 9), associated with the monk Yūsei (宥盛), designated on March 31, 2009. == location == * '''Address:''' 973 Kawanishi, Kotohira, Nakatado District, Kagawa Prefecture, Japan (香川県仲多度郡琴平町字川西973番地) == Pilgrimage == * '''Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage''' ''' 15: {{ill|Kenshō-ji (Marugame)|ja|顕正寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenshō-ji (Marugame)|1=Kenshō-ji (Marugame)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665121}} → '''16: Matsuo-ji''' → 17: {{ill|Butsumo-in|ja|仏母院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Butsumo-in|1=Butsumoin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11377550}} == References == === Notes === {{Notelist}} === [[commons:Category:Matsuo-ji (Kotohira)]][[en:Matsuo-ji]][[ja:松尾寺 (香川県琴平町)]] [[commons:Category:Matsuo-ji (Kotohira)]][[en:Matsuo-ji]][[ja:松尾寺 (香川県琴平町)]] [[sn:commons:Category:Matsuo-ji (Kotohira)]][[en:Matsuo-ji]][[ja:松尾寺 (香川県琴平町)]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Matuozi02.jpg|religious_affiliation={{ill|Kōyasan Shingon-shū|WD=Q3077357}}|prefecture={{ill|Kotohira|WD=Q1271483}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.185778|N|133.818389|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|deity={{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|WD=Q11644537}}|part_of={{ill|Shin-Shikoku Mandara Pilgrimage|WD=Q11501519}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11529610]] Wikidata name: Matsuo-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:20, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Japanese temple infobox | Name = Matsuo-ji (松尾寺) | image = [[File:Matuozi02.jpg|250px]]
Main Hall | location = [[:en:Kotohira, Kagawa|Kotohira, Kagawa]], [[:en:Nakatado District, Kagawa|Nakatado District, Kagawa]], [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{Coord|34|11|8.8|N|133|49|6.2|E}} | Mount name = Zōzusan (象頭山) | Sect = [[:en:Kōyasan Shingon-shū|Kōyasan Shingon-shū]] | Main deity = {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}} | Founded = [[:en:Taiho|Taihō era]] (701–704 AD) | Founder = [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]] | Official name = Zōzusan Fumon-in Matsuo-ji (象頭山 普門院 松尾寺) | Pilgrimage = {{ill|Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|ja|新四国曼荼羅霊場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|1=Shin-Shikoku Mandara Pilgrimage|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11501519}} No.16 }} '''Matsuo-ji''' (松尾寺) is a [[:en:Kōyasan Shingon-shū|Kōyasan Shingon-shū]] temple located in [[:en:Kotohira, Kagawa|Kotohira, Kagawa]], [[:en:Nakatado District, Kagawa|Nakatado District, Kagawa]], [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]]. The temple's mountain name (山号) is Zōzusan (象頭山), and its cloister name (院号) is Fumon-in (普門院). The principal deity is {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}}, and it is the 16th temple in the {{ill|Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|ja|新四国曼荼羅霊場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|1=Shin-Shikoku Mandara Pilgrimage|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11501519}}. '''Temple hymn (Goeika, ご詠歌):''' *"Miagureba / kokoro mo kiyoki / Zōzusan / kin no gohei ni / kami zo masumasu"* (*"Looking up, / even the heart is purified, / Zōzusan, / with golden gohei, / the deity is ever-present."*) == History == [[File:Matuoji 20191125 02.jpg|thumb|180px|Sanmon (temple gate)]] According to the temple's legend, Matsuo-ji was founded in the [[:en:Taiho|Taihō era]] (701–704 AD) when [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]], the founder of [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendō]], climbed Mount Zōzusan. It is said that he received a divine revelation from {{ill|Kumbhīra (mythology)|ja|マカラ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumbhīra (mythology)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to マカラ (神話)|1=Makara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1136842}}, a protective deity (護法善神) dwelling in [[:en:Vulture Peak|Vulture Peak]] in India, which later became associated with [[:en:Konpira Gongen|Konpira Gongen]].Short history of Matsuo-ji However, many historical records were lost during the invasion of [[:en:Chōsokabe Motochika|Chōsokabe Motochika]] into [[:en:Sanuki Province|Sanuki Province]]. The earliest records of Konpira Gongen appear only in the early modern period. Before that, Matsuo-ji had its main hall at the current site of Hongū (本宮), enshrining an Eleven-Headed Kannon (十一面観音菩薩) as its principal deity. A shrine to the Thirty Guardian Deities (三十番神) was also located nearby. During the late 16th century, Matsuo-ji underwent significant transformations. A monk named Yūga (宥雅), a disciple of [[:en:Zentsū-ji|Zentsū-ji]], became the temple's abbot and founded Konkō-in (金光院). In 1573, he established a hall for Konpira Gongen (金毘羅堂) at the foot of the temple complex, separate from the original temple structure. In 1579, when [[:en:Chōsokabe Motochika|Chōsokabe Motochika]] sought to control religious institutions in Sanuki, Yūga fled, and the Chōsokabe forces appointed the monk Yūgen (宥厳) as the new abbot. During the Edo period, Konpira Gongen's faith expanded, and Matsuo-ji became a prominent center of pilgrimage. In 1813, the temple's Yakushi Hall (薬師堂) was rebuilt into a grand structure, housing a [[:en:Bhaisajyaguru|Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha]] statue and the Twelve Divine Generals (薬師十二神将). The temple flourished under the patronage of various regional rulers, and Konpira shrines spread throughout Japan. === Meiji Restoration and the Separation of Shinto and Buddhism === During the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) policies led to significant changes at Matsuo-ji. The temple complex was restructured: - The mountain name was changed from Zōzusan (象頭山) to Kotohirayama (琴平山). - The temple village was renamed from Konpira Village (金毘羅村) to Kotohira Village (琴平村). - The shrine was renamed from Konpira Gongen (金毘羅大権現) to [[:en:Kotohira-gū|Kotohira-gū]]. The temple itself was largely dismantled, with only Fumon-in (普門院) remaining under the name Matsuo-ji. Many Buddhist structures were converted into Shinto shrines or destroyed. However, some Buddhist artifacts, such as a Kamakura-period statue of {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}}, were preserved and enshrined in the temple. == Temple Grounds == [[File:Matuojo 20191125 01.jpg|thumb|180px|Main Gate (Sanmon)]] The temple complex includes the following structures: * Sanmon (temple gate) * Main Hall (本堂) * Bell Tower (鐘楼堂) * Kuri (庫裡, temple living quarters) == Cultural Properties == '''Kagawa Prefecture Designated Cultural Properties''' * '''Seated Statue of Kōbō Daishi''' (木造弘法大師座像) – Created in 1319 (Bunpō 3), made of cypress wood (檜材), designated on March 31, 2009. * '''Wooden Five-Ring Pagoda''' (木造五輪塔) – Contains an inscription dated 1604 (Keichō 9), associated with the monk Yūsei (宥盛), designated on March 31, 2009. == location == * '''Address:''' 973 Kawanishi, Kotohira, Nakatado District, Kagawa Prefecture, Japan (香川県仲多度郡琴平町字川西973番地) == Pilgrimage == * '''Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage''' ''' 15: {{ill|Kenshō-ji (Marugame)|ja|顕正寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenshō-ji (Marugame)|1=Kenshō-ji (Marugame)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665121}} → '''16: Matsuo-ji''' → 17: {{ill|Butsumo-in|ja|仏母院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Butsumo-in|1=Butsumoin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11377550}} == References == === Notes === {{Notelist}} === Sources === {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[:en:Konpira Gongen|Konpira Gongen]] == External Links == * [http://www.mandala88.com/ Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage Website] {{translated page|ja|松尾寺 (香川県琴平町)|version=98510282|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Buddhist temples in Kagawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Kōyasan Shingon temples]] Main Hall | location = [[:en:Kotohira, Kagawa|Kotohira, Kagawa]], [[:en:Nakatado District, Kagawa|Nakatado District, Kagawa]], [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{Coord|34|11|8.8|N|133|49|6.2|E}} | Mount name = Zōzusan (象頭山) | Sect = [[:en:Kōyasan Shingon-shū|Kōyasan Shingon-shū]] | Main deity = {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}} | Founded = [[:en:Taiho|Taihō era]] (701–704 AD) | Founder = [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]] | Official name = Zōzusan Fumon-in Matsuo-ji (象頭山 普門院 松尾寺) | Pilgrimage = {{ill|Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|ja|新四国曼荼羅霊場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|1=Shin-Shikoku Mandara Pilgrimage|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11501519}} No.16 }} '''Matsuo-ji''' (松尾寺) is a [[:en:Kōyasan Shingon-shū|Kōyasan Shingon-shū]] temple located in [[:en:Kotohira, Kagawa|Kotohira, Kagawa]], [[:en:Nakatado District, Kagawa|Nakatado District, Kagawa]], [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]]. The temple's mountain name (山号) is Zōzusan (象頭山), and its cloister name (院号) is Fumon-in (普門院). The principal deity is {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}}, and it is the 16th temple in the {{ill|Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|ja|新四国曼荼羅霊場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|1=Shin-Shikoku Mandara Pilgrimage|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11501519}}. '''Temple hymn (Goeika, ご詠歌):''' *"Miagureba / kokoro mo kiyoki / Zōzusan / kin no gohei ni / kami zo masumasu"* (*"Looking up, / even the heart is purified, / Zōzusan, / with golden gohei, / the deity is ever-present."*) == History == [[File:Matuoji 20191125 02.jpg|thumb|180px|Sanmon (temple gate)]] According to the temple's legend, Matsuo-ji was founded in the [[:en:Taiho|Taihō era]] (701–704 AD) when [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]], the founder of [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendō]], climbed Mount Zōzusan. It is said that he received a divine revelation from {{ill|Kumbhīra (mythology)|ja|マカラ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumbhīra (mythology)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to マカラ (神話)|1=Makara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1136842}}, a protective deity (護法善神) dwelling in [[:en:Vulture Peak|Vulture Peak]] in India, which later became associated with [[:en:Konpira Gongen|Konpira Gongen]].Short history of Matsuo-ji However, many historical records were lost during the invasion of [[:en:Chōsokabe Motochika|Chōsokabe Motochika]] into [[:en:Sanuki Province|Sanuki Province]]. The earliest records of Konpira Gongen appear only in the early modern period. Before that, Matsuo-ji had its main hall at the current site of Hongū (本宮), enshrining an Eleven-Headed Kannon (十一面観音菩薩) as its principal deity. A shrine to the Thirty Guardian Deities (三十番神) was also located nearby. During the late 16th century, Matsuo-ji underwent significant transformations. A monk named Yūga (宥雅), a disciple of [[:en:Zentsū-ji|Zentsū-ji]], became the temple's abbot and founded Konkō-in (金光院). In 1573, he established a hall for Konpira Gongen (金毘羅堂) at the foot of the temple complex, separate from the original temple structure. In 1579, when [[:en:Chōsokabe Motochika|Chōsokabe Motochika]] sought to control religious institutions in Sanuki, Yūga fled, and the Chōsokabe forces appointed the monk Yūgen (宥厳) as the new abbot. During the Edo period, Konpira Gongen's faith expanded, and Matsuo-ji became a prominent center of pilgrimage. In 1813, the temple's Yakushi Hall (薬師堂) was rebuilt into a grand structure, housing a [[:en:Bhaisajyaguru|Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha]] statue and the Twelve Divine Generals (薬師十二神将). The temple flourished under the patronage of various regional rulers, and Konpira shrines spread throughout Japan. === Meiji Restoration and the Separation of Shinto and Buddhism === During the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) policies led to significant changes at Matsuo-ji. The temple complex was restructured: - The mountain name was changed from Zōzusan (象頭山) to Kotohirayama (琴平山). - The temple village was renamed from Konpira Village (金毘羅村) to Kotohira Village (琴平村). - The shrine was renamed from Konpira Gongen (金毘羅大権現) to [[:en:Kotohira-gū|Kotohira-gū]]. The temple itself was largely dismantled, with only Fumon-in (普門院) remaining under the name Matsuo-ji. Many Buddhist structures were converted into Shinto shrines or destroyed. However, some Buddhist artifacts, such as a Kamakura-period statue of {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}}, were preserved and enshrined in the temple. == Temple Grounds == [[File:Matuojo 20191125 01.jpg|thumb|180px|Main Gate (Sanmon)]] The temple complex includes the following structures: * Sanmon (temple gate) * Main Hall (本堂) * Bell Tower (鐘楼堂) * Kuri (庫裡, temple living quarters) == Cultural Properties == '''Kagawa Prefecture Designated Cultural Properties''' * '''Seated Statue of Kōbō Daishi''' (木造弘法大師座像) – Created in 1319 (Bunpō 3), made of cypress wood (檜材), designated on March 31, 2009. * '''Wooden Five-Ring Pagoda''' (木造五輪塔) – Contains an inscription dated 1604 (Keichō 9), associated with the monk Yūsei (宥盛), designated on March 31, 2009. == location == * '''Address:''' 973 Kawanishi, Kotohira, Nakatado District, Kagawa Prefecture, Japan (香川県仲多度郡琴平町字川西973番地) == Pilgrimage == * '''Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage''' ''' 15: {{ill|Kenshō-ji (Marugame)|ja|顕正寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenshō-ji (Marugame)|1=Kenshō-ji (Marugame)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665121}} → '''16: Matsuo-ji''' → 17: {{ill|Butsumo-in|ja|仏母院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Butsumo-in|1=Butsumoin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11377550}} == References == === Notes === {{Notelist}} === Hall (本堂) * Bell Tower (鐘楼堂) * Kuri (庫裡, temple living quarters) == Cultural Properties == '''Kagawa Prefecture Designated Cultural Properties''' * '''Seated Statue of Kōbō Daishi''' (木造弘法大師座像) – Created in 1319 (Bunpō 3), made of cypress wood (檜材), designated on March 31, 2009. * '''Wooden Five-Ring Pagoda''' (木造五輪塔) – Contains an inscription dated 1604 (Keichō 9), associated with the monk Yūsei (宥盛), designated on March 31, 2009. == location == * '''Address:''' 973 Kawanishi, Kotohira, Nakatado District, Kagawa Prefecture, Japan (香川県仲多度郡琴平町字川西973番地) == Pilgrimage == * '''Shikoku [[commons:Category:Matsuo-ji (Kotohira)]][[en:Matsuo-ji]][[ja:松尾寺 (香川県琴平町)]] [[commons:Category:Matsuo-ji (Kotohira)]][[en:Matsuo-ji]][[ja:松尾寺 (香川県琴平町)]] [[sn:commons:Category:Matsuo-ji (Kotohira)]][[en:Matsuo-ji]][[ja:松尾寺 (香川県琴平町)]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Matuozi02.jpg|religious_affiliation={{ill|Kōyasan Shingon-shū|WD=Q3077357}}|prefecture={{ill|Kotohira|WD=Q1271483}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.185778|N|133.818389|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|deity={{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|WD=Q11644537}}|part_of={{ill|Shin-Shikoku Mandara Pilgrimage|WD=Q11501519}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11529610]] Wikidata name: Matsuo-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:20, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Japanese temple infobox | Name = Matsuo-ji (松尾寺) | image = [[File:Matuozi02.jpg|250px]]
Main Hall | location = [[:en:Kotohira, Kagawa|Kotohira, Kagawa]], [[:en:Nakatado District, Kagawa|Nakatado District, Kagawa]], [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{Coord|34|11|8.8|N|133|49|6.2|E}} | Mount name = Zōzusan (象頭山) | Sect = [[:en:Kōyasan Shingon-shū|Kōyasan Shingon-shū]] | Main deity = {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}} | Founded = [[:en:Taiho|Taihō era]] (701–704 AD) | Founder = [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]] | Official name = Zōzusan Fumon-in Matsuo-ji (象頭山 普門院 松尾寺) | Pilgrimage = {{ill|Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|ja|新四国曼荼羅霊場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|1=Shin-Shikoku Mandara Pilgrimage|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11501519}} No.16 }} '''Matsuo-ji''' (松尾寺) is a [[:en:Kōyasan Shingon-shū|Kōyasan Shingon-shū]] temple located in [[:en:Kotohira, Kagawa|Kotohira, Kagawa]], [[:en:Nakatado District, Kagawa|Nakatado District, Kagawa]], [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]]. The temple's mountain name (山号) is Zōzusan (象頭山), and its cloister name (院号) is Fumon-in (普門院). The principal deity is {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}}, and it is the 16th temple in the {{ill|Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|ja|新四国曼荼羅霊場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|1=Shin-Shikoku Mandara Pilgrimage|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11501519}}. '''Temple hymn (Goeika, ご詠歌):''' *"Miagureba / kokoro mo kiyoki / Zōzusan / kin no gohei ni / kami zo masumasu"* (*"Looking up, / even the heart is purified, / Zōzusan, / with golden gohei, / the deity is ever-present."*) == History == [[File:Matuoji 20191125 02.jpg|thumb|180px|Sanmon (temple gate)]] According to the temple's legend, Matsuo-ji was founded in the [[:en:Taiho|Taihō era]] (701–704 AD) when [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]], the founder of [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendō]], climbed Mount Zōzusan. It is said that he received a divine revelation from {{ill|Kumbhīra (mythology)|ja|マカラ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumbhīra (mythology)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to マカラ (神話)|1=Makara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1136842}}, a protective deity (護法善神) dwelling in [[:en:Vulture Peak|Vulture Peak]] in India, which later became associated with [[:en:Konpira Gongen|Konpira Gongen]].Short history of Matsuo-ji However, many historical records were lost during the invasion of [[:en:Chōsokabe Motochika|Chōsokabe Motochika]] into [[:en:Sanuki Province|Sanuki Province]]. The earliest records of Konpira Gongen appear only in the early modern period. Before that, Matsuo-ji had its main hall at the current site of Hongū (本宮), enshrining an Eleven-Headed Kannon (十一面観音菩薩) as its principal deity. A shrine to the Thirty Guardian Deities (三十番神) was also located nearby. During the late 16th century, Matsuo-ji underwent significant transformations. A monk named Yūga (宥雅), a disciple of [[:en:Zentsū-ji|Zentsū-ji]], became the temple's abbot and founded Konkō-in (金光院). In 1573, he established a hall for Konpira Gongen (金毘羅堂) at the foot of the temple complex, separate from the original temple structure. In 1579, when [[:en:Chōsokabe Motochika|Chōsokabe Motochika]] sought to control religious institutions in Sanuki, Yūga fled, and the Chōsokabe forces appointed the monk Yūgen (宥厳) as the new abbot. During the Edo period, Konpira Gongen's faith expanded, and Matsuo-ji became a prominent center of pilgrimage. In 1813, the temple's Yakushi Hall (薬師堂) was rebuilt into a grand structure, housing a [[:en:Bhaisajyaguru|Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha]] statue and the Twelve Divine Generals (薬師十二神将). The temple flourished under the patronage of various regional rulers, and Konpira shrines spread throughout Japan. === Meiji Restoration and the Separation of Shinto and Buddhism === During the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) policies led to significant changes at Matsuo-ji. The temple complex was restructured: - The mountain name was changed from Zōzusan (象頭山) to Kotohirayama (琴平山). - The temple village was renamed from Konpira Village (金毘羅村) to Kotohira Village (琴平村). - The shrine was renamed from Konpira Gongen (金毘羅大権現) to [[:en:Kotohira-gū|Kotohira-gū]]. The temple itself was largely dismantled, with only Fumon-in (普門院) remaining under the name Matsuo-ji. Many Buddhist structures were converted into Shinto shrines or destroyed. However, some Buddhist artifacts, such as a Kamakura-period statue of {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}}, were preserved and enshrined in the temple. == Temple Grounds == [[File:Matuojo 20191125 01.jpg|thumb|180px|Main Gate (Sanmon)]] The temple complex includes the following structures: * Sanmon (temple gate) * Main Hall (本堂) * Bell Tower (鐘楼堂) * Kuri (庫裡, temple living quarters) == Cultural Properties == '''Kagawa Prefecture Designated Cultural Properties''' * '''Seated Statue of Kōbō Daishi''' (木造弘法大師座像) – Created in 1319 (Bunpō 3), made of cypress wood (檜材), designated on March 31, 2009. * '''Wooden Five-Ring Pagoda''' (木造五輪塔) – Contains an inscription dated 1604 (Keichō 9), associated with the monk Yūsei (宥盛), designated on March 31, 2009. == location == * '''Address:''' 973 Kawanishi, Kotohira, Nakatado District, Kagawa Prefecture, Japan (香川県仲多度郡琴平町字川西973番地) == Pilgrimage == * '''Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage''' ''' 15: {{ill|Kenshō-ji (Marugame)|ja|顕正寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenshō-ji (Marugame)|1=Kenshō-ji (Marugame)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665121}} → '''16: Matsuo-ji''' → 17: {{ill|Butsumo-in|ja|仏母院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Butsumo-in|1=Butsumoin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11377550}} == References == === Notes === {{Notelist}} ===
